title: Vägen till bra feedback på jobbet
text:
# The Path to Good Feedback at Work

During my time in school, feedback was a given. Especially in design education, design critique was a natural part of the process. It was painful to see many late nights of hard work being questioned and picked apart. It mattered more that I could explain my thinking than that the result looked "nice." I couldn't hide a bad idea behind a sleek facade and a cool typeface. The greatest reward was the realization that the design actually improved and I myself became more critical and proactive in my thinking. Suddenly I could avoid many pitfalls that I had previously happily fallen into.

Judge my surprise when I entered the working world and realized that most companies lack a healthy feedback culture. All feedback is typically saved for development conversations, which happen once a year, and are never followed up on. As employees (designers), we're expected to be creative, innovative, high-performing and productive. We should think outside the box, learn new things and skills, but without making any (costly) mistakes. All this without receiving continuous, constructive feedback.

So what is the reason we're so afraid of feedback? How can it be that many people would rather drive an entire project into the ditch than find out in time that they've done something wrong? Feedback is difficult—both to give, to receive, to ask for, and to offer. It's not easy when you're a new employee, an individual in the middle of the hierarchy trying to influence a feedback-skeptical organization. The best approach is to start gently and ask for feedback yourself. Don't use the word criticism, not even constructive criticism. Even the word feedback can be very loaded and cause people to slowly back out of the room. Package the feedback session as improvement suggestions, finding problems, iterating, or brainstorming. Say that you've gotten stuck with an idea, sketch, or prototype and need help moving forward. If you want feedback on how to improve your design, the last thing you want to hear is "nice" or "I don't know anything about design." Preferably have a few concrete questions in case the conversation needs a nudge in the right direction. For example, "How can I make the design feel more educational?" or "Is there anything in the design that feels unclear?"

Repeat this procedure until it feels completely natural for both you and your colleagues. Show that you appreciate others' opinions even if you disagree or don't make the suggested changes. The purpose is for you to gain a broader perspective, not a bug fix list. Hopefully you've planted a seed for a thriving feedback culture. Now you can carefully offer your help when your colleagues get stuck (i.e., need feedback).

Some prerequisites for a constructive feedback culture:

**Framework:**
Set some simple rules and guidelines for how you want feedback to function. How and when it's delivered, what it should contain, what tone it needs to have, and so on. It should also be quick and easy to arrange a session. You can also have a collection of questions that often come up during your sessions. This can help inexperienced people get started.

**Safety:**
It can be very difficult to receive feedback, even if it's constructive and well-formulated. The recipient needs to feel safe and know that the feedback is honest, well-intentioned, and meant to help. Time and place are just as important as the content. It should be relaxed, preferably one-on-one or in a closed setting where what's said doesn't take on a life of its own.

**Trust:**
Who gives the feedback is just as important as what is said. Is it some guy on Flashback going off the rails or is it your best friend giving you heartfelt advice? You can still listen to both, but the likelihood is higher that you'll take feedback from someone you trust, look up to, and respect.

**Routine:**
Feedback is like exercise. It gets easier and better the more often you do it. Short and frequent is better than long and rare. Get feedback as a natural part of every partial delivery, check-in, or presentation.

How does it work at your company? What works well and what can be improved?

title: Being a designer is not a joke
text:
Being a designer is not a joke
Being a designer is not a joke
Created on 2019-11-28 10:49
Published on 2019-11-28 11:11
How many designers does it take to change a light bulb?
- Does it have to be a light bulb?
Have you heard it before? Many of my (non designer) colleagues laughed with recognition. They see designers as children with crayons, drawing pretty pictures, asking silly questions and don’t really producing anything real. When confronted with a single, mundane task, designers get stuck. And the joke visualize that in a very striking and funny way.
I have a totally different interpretation of that joke, and unfortunately it’s not funny at all. Sorry. To me it's the definition of being a designer and design thinker. We designers avoid doings things on autopilot, without reflecting over the purpose. We start by asking questions: "Can it be done in a different, more efficient, accessible, sustainable or fun way? What is the purpose of changing the bulb? Who is going to use it, when, how and why? What kind of light should the bulb produce? Can it do anything more, like change color, intensity or blink? Should it behave differently in the morning, evening, during the night or when nobody is at home? Are there any other possible sources of light?
This base of understanding is source of our inspiration. Then we start prototyping, testing and observing. Without understanding the problem we can not evaluate the result. This is the power of design. To push thing forward and open new possibilities where others see a single solution.
Finding answers to all those question is fascinating, inspiring and drives innovation. But it takes time. Because time is a limited resource, people and companies don’t want to waste it. Your clients wants to see result and final art screenshots, not personas and post-its on a whiteboard. Your jobb is to educate, communicate and involve your boss, clients and colleagues in your design process. Show that those seemingly non productive actions are a vital part of the final solution. Make them co-creators and a part of the process.
The goal is to make them to ask the question: Does it have to be a light bulb?

"DS is not science with hard data to prove or disapprove your hypothesis. It’s a fast experiment that tests one scenario and validates it with real users. title: Vart tog all (B2C) kärlek vägen?
text:
# Where Did All the (B2C) Love Go?

In the beginning, everything is always so beautiful and romantic. I'm constantly courted, I feel seen and understood, sweet promises are whispered in my ear. "Come to us, we'll solve all your problems, we promise. We're here for you, today, tomorrow and forever." Eventually I fall for the temptation, hoping that this time it will actually work.

But as soon as I've signed the contract, something happens. Or rather, that's just it—nothing happens. All these expectations, the magic I was promised in the advertisements, the seductive words from the salesman's lips. It comes to nothing. Just emptiness and silence. Maybe the occasional anonymous monthly letter, advertising that has nothing to do with me, or a time-consuming and boring survey that gives nothing back.

Why does it always end up like this? Is it really impossible for a company to have a meaningful relationship with its customers? I understand that I'm not the only one, of course they can see others, but I still want to feel seen and special. I want them to remember my choices, preferences and history. I want them to reward me for my loyalty, to discover and fix things themselves that don't work or could be improved. If I never use all my data or broadband speed, why not give me a signal and offer me something that better suits my needs? And ALL the banks out there. Why, why why don't I get notified that there's insufficient coverage on my account when a bill is about to be withdrawn? When I then have to pay the hefty reminder fee to CSN, I'm just as angry at myself as I am at you. Why didn't you say anything when you knew? Why did you just look away and think it's not your responsibility? I feel deceived. I gave you my trust and you betray it again and again.

Then there's the matter of breaking up in a civilized way. It can't come as a shock that I've found someone else after being completely ignored for several years. That you then come with a special offer I don't want to miss just feels desperate and calculating. Do you really want to show that it's sad? Ask why I'm choosing to switch, what you could do better, what I was missing or what the competitor did better? The only thing this text message shows is that loyalty doesn't pay off—I'm only rewarded at the beginning of the relationship and when I cancel. It feels like a very dysfunctional relationship where one party has to threaten to break up as soon as something doesn't work.

It doesn't have to be that bloody difficult. You have all my information, you know where my mailbox is, what I've bought, what I like and don't like. Grocery stores should understand from my history that I'm vegetarian and probably don't appreciate ads with dead animals on sale. I don't want to choose from the left menu between vegetarian or organic. I want both. I don't care if it doesn't work with your platform or if the demographic "eco-vegetarian" is missing from your user profiles. Do it right.

It's time for companies to take relationship building seriously. Not with consumption-driven tempting prices, misdirected loyalty programs or flashy advertising campaigns. If you want my loyalty, my data and my money, you need to know who I am, what I want and need. Only then can our relationship live happily ever after.

Bra praktisk genomgång för hur man genomför DS på distans. Tack AJ&Smart."

title: Bli en bättre designer med DISC
text:
# Become a Better Designer with DISC

Become a Better Designer with DISC
Created on 2020-04-30 10:46
Published on 2020-04-30 11:12

To become a truly good designer requires more than creative ideas and nice sketches. The real challenge lies in communicating your work in the right way and selling it to your team, colleagues, or clients. Otherwise, your design remains nothing but sketches on your laptop.

A classic mistake I made as a junior designer was believing that good design speaks for itself. Once the sketches were done, I thought I was finished. I interpreted every question as criticism and proof that others didn't understand anything about design. It turned out that I was the one who didn't understand anything about communication. Now, a bit older and wiser, I've found new tools to design communication itself. With a small twist, the popular DISC model can ensure that your ideas actually get through.

What is DISC?

At its core, the DISC model is a four-quadrant framework that describes four different personality traits. Usually, a person has a combination of two quadrants where one is slightly more pronounced. This gives you 16 different personality types and adds more depth to the analysis. The model shows how the different traits interact and complement each other. The opposite sides, in other words, mirror opposite personality traits.

There are three areas where DISC can help you become a better designer and communicator.

Get to Know Yourself

It's difficult to communicate with others if you don't get to know yourself first. What are my strengths and weaknesses, what comes easy and what is hard, what's fun and what is genuinely tedious? By writing it down, you make it extra clear and certain patterns start to emerge. Then I can plan ahead and compensate for upcoming difficulties. Personally, I know I have strong yellow influential tendencies. Everything involving creativity, big visions, or workshops I handle without problems. But if I have to sit alone with monotonous tasks requiring careful planning and lots of details, then I'm going to struggle. If I know what's coming, I can prepare myself properly and make sure I reach the finish line.

Get to Know Others

Why is it so easy to collaborate with some people and almost impossible to get along with others? My big mistake was assuming that others were just like me. What was important to me should be equally important to them. I expected to be met with the same enthusiasm, openness, and energy that I gave. When I didn't get that, I felt hurt, disappointed, and thought the other person was disengaged or downright unfriendly.

Here it's about leaving your ego at home. Respect and welcome differences as much as common ground. It's rare that you get new perspectives from someone who thinks and feels exactly like you do. Understand what matters to the other party. If you want to convince an analytical person, you won't get far with spontaneous creativity and gut feeling. Here you need to show that you have control of the situation and have thought everything through carefully. Instead of mood boards, present material with hard facts, research, and statistics. Pure opinion without evidence is completely irrelevant.

The Whole Cost Circle in the Project

There is no part that is better or worse—they complement each other in an excellent way. Make sure you also think from these different perspectives. Focus on areas that are important to the person you're talking to. Here are some examples of questions from certain color groups.

The red section is focused on action and results.

What's the timeline and what are the deadlines? What's the next step, when should it be done, etc.?
What roles exist in the project and who is responsible for what?
What results are we expected to achieve? What is the project expected to deliver, improve, optimize, etc.?

The yellow section is about creativity and inspiration.

What's innovative about the project? What's the creative height, innovation power, etc.?
What's the big vision? What has improved once the project is complete?
Why should I, the customer, the people care?
How should we communicate and package our message?

The green section is about thoughtfulness, humanity, and purpose-orientation.

How does the project relate to sustainability, environment, ethics, etc.?
In what way will the project make the world, society better, or make people happier and more productive?
How will we work together as a team?

The blue section concerns the logical and analytical.

What evidence do we have for our decisions?
What measurable goals do we have and how will we measure if we've succeeded?
Do we have the resources (time, money, personnel, competence, space, contacts, knowledge, experience, authority, muscle, etc.) required to succeed?
What are the risks and costs?

The DISC model is simple to understand and use as a starting point. But remember that a model is just a model. Neither you, your colleagues, nor your customers ARE yellow or red. People don't behave the same way in every situation; they aren't static but constantly developing. Reality is, fortunately, much more complex and exciting than a colorful ball.

Good luck with your next project.

title: Bli en bättre designer. Börja  skriva.
text:
# Become a Better Designer. Start Writing.

Created on 2020-11-02 20:14
Published on 2020-11-02 20:59

Seth Godin writes a blog post every day. Impressive, perhaps something I should try. But I know myself. Even once a week is a challenge. I'm simply not good at perseverance and long-term investments. Just because it's difficult doesn't mean it's impossible or that I'm not going to try (again). Quite the opposite. I'm going to prove it can be done. If not this time, then next.

To increase my chances, I'm going to give myself some good advice along the way, based on my previous failed attempts.

**Write for yourself.**

Before your fingers even reach the keyboard, the first mental barrier arrives. "What do I have to say that hasn't already been said by people who are smarter, more experienced, more famous, and more articulate than I am?" There's hardly a shortage of blogs, podcasts, articles, tweets, videos, streams about everything under the sun. It's even seen as a virtue to keep quiet and not contribute to the noise. My answer: Don't compare yourself to others. Do it for yourself. Forget about likes and followers. It's nice if someone reads what you write, but the goal is to write what you want to write about, not what gets the most likes.

**Dig where you stand.**

I like the motto: "Start where you are, work with what you have, and do everything you can." Wherever you are in life, your career, or your mental maturity, you have something to say. You don't need to be an expert, but a strong interest in the subject is worth recommending. Why should the reader care if you don't? Are you a beginner in the field? Embrace that perspective. Write how you see things with completely fresh eyes. That's something a senior expert can't recreate.

**Let's follow your journey.**

If you blog regularly (as I hopefully will soon be doing), your posts become over time a map of your thoughts and ideas—where you've been and where you're headed. Hopefully, you'll find your earlier posts quite bad, with opinions you might not share anymore. Congratulations, you've developed. Also dare to explore areas you don't understand and ask questions you don't have answers to. Just listening to experts with fixed opinions is quite tedious.

**Everyone sucks at first.**

If you haven't written much before, it will feel stiff at first, take a long time, and honestly be quite bad. Every first draft is terrible. No panic. Read through what you've written, edit it, and rewrite. But don't get stuck. If the post turns out bad, boring, or clunky, focus on the next one. Fortunately, the pixels in your computer don't run out. If you're unsure whether you should take it to the next level, ask for feedback from people you look up to. Then it's just about continuing to write. No matter what.

**Concretize your thoughts.**

It's much easier to determine if an idea is good or bad when you write it down. It becomes immediately more concrete and won't risk being forgotten. If you develop the idea into a longer text, it also gets placed in context with other thoughts and ideas. Suddenly patterns emerge that you wouldn't have thought of otherwise. The idea becomes an insight.

**Writing as a workout.**

By writing regularly, you exercise your verbal ability. You'll express yourself in a more structured and educational way, know what you want to say and how to convey it. Everything you write—from emails to reports and documentation—will get a real boost. Think of all the tedious and incoherent reports you've been forced to read. Don't contribute to that misery.

**Build a verbal portfolio.**

Good design is just as much about the thinking behind the work as the result itself. Thinking about design, discussing it, and critiquing it is just as valuable (if not more so) as working with pixels. If you're looking for a job, new assignments, or a step up in your career, a blog, vlog, or podcast is a brilliant way to show what you stand for, how you think, and how you see the big picture.

**Set aside time.**

A fixed time or short sessions here and there? Find what works best for you. Count on it taking time, at least at first. Set a deadline or timeframe so you don't overwork. Prioritize quantity over quality. The best workout is the one that actually happens. ;-)

I like the meta of writing about how to get started with writing in order to get started with my own writing. To wrap up, I wish us both good luck and I'm already looking forward to what we're going to accomplish.
Become part of our awesome CS team, with many exciting customers and a product that creates real value.

Ha! I was afraid that my blogging was just a passing phase. But like the internet, or real flies, I'm here to stay. And to talk about this and that.

TGIF ;-)

title: Lära sig genom att lära ut, eller hur jag slutade vara en glad idiot.
text:
# Learning by Teaching, or How I Stopped Being a Blissfully Ignorant Fool

Created on 2020-11-13 10:03
Published on 2020-11-13 10:33

That sounded incredibly paradoxical, I thought when I first heard the claim. How can you teach something you don't know? I trust someone with solid experience more than a green beginner.

But there's something to it. Knowing something yourself is one thing, and being able to explain it to someone else is quite another. Take directions as an example. You know exactly how to get to the nearest subway station or grocery store. But when you need to explain it to a lost person, what was obvious suddenly becomes less obvious. How many intersections would you pass and what was the name of that crossing street anyway?

An explanation requires fundamental understanding as well as pedagogical structure and organization. If you want someone else to understand something, you need to start from the beginning, step by step at a reasonable pace, preferably with examples and anchor it in reality or something your "student" already knows. This isn't something I invented. It's a popular pedagogical method, classified under collaborative learning, where you learn together, from and with each other.

But I certainly had no idea about any of this when I agreed to teach the course User-Centered Design at Nackademin. I knew my stuff about UCD and UX, after all—I'd worked with it for years. And Design Thinking, well, I was familiar with that. I'd seen the model countless times in different forms and variations.

How hard could it be?

Looking back now, I'm glad I didn't know how little I knew. I was at the bottom of the knowledge pyramid, a blissfully ignorant fool unaware of my incompetence. "How hard could it be? It's just a matter of turning my knowledge into some slides."

Famous last words. It took me a month of intensive studying and reviewing before I started putting all the puzzle pieces together and slowly climbing the pyramid. Suddenly there was context; what had previously been loose rattling puzzle pieces formed into a clear picture. There was cause and effect, differences and similarities, before and after. Now I was ready. And yes, it was difficult, but absolutely worth it.

So what has this learning taught me?

You don't need to be an expert to teach. It could just be running a workshop, giving a presentation, or hosting a lunch and learn.

It takes an insane amount of time to create material and plan. So start early and let it take time. This is where you discover any gaps in your knowledge.

Prioritize and create focus. What's important, what comes first, what should people take with them? How does it all connect?

Pedagogy and design have a lot in common. As a teacher, you have the opportunity to design others' future knowledge. How cool is that?

Increased confidence, both in the subject matter, but also in being the one who leads, takes responsibility, and sets the agenda. Above all, overcoming the Swedes' greatest fear—"speaking in front of people." (Actually, it's number two; number one is death).

Learning in a group. All the ideas, questions, suggestions, and discussions with the students are incredibly inspiring.

Disclaimer: I'm not a trained teacher, I lack formal teacher training and a "proper" teaching job. I work full-time as a product designer and the course at Nackademin is a side project. If I were to call myself something pedagogical, it would be course leader. What I do have, however, is a great interest in pedagogy and sharing my knowledge and experiences.

title: I just don’t want to be cool
text:
I just don't want to be cool
I just don't want to be cool
Created on 2020-11-20 09:24
Published on 2020-11-20 09:41

There's a lot of discussion about what UX is and isn't. But there's one thing nobody mentions. UX isn't particularly cool. Sure, everyone talks about it. UX is trending in job postings and new education programs are popping up like flowers in a summer meadow. But it lacks all kinds of flair and street cred. It's like being a naprapath—you help people in need but nobody makes action movies about you, unlike being a police officer. I'm not saying this because I don't like UX, quite the opposite—I see it as a positive quality and a superpower.

I've been on the other side, which pure UX practitioners regard with equal parts skepticism and envy. I've had the roles of graphic designer, Art Director, and UI designer. I've poured my soul into my visual design, slavishly followed all the trends, Dribbble accounts, typography blogs, and super-sleek ad campaigns. I've done my best to transform dull wireframe sketches into a living product with its own personality that created a rewarding and inspiring conversation with users. I wanted to touch people and create a link between the screen and humanity.

Unfortunately, there was a downside to this cool, creative existence. It was called reality, and it crashed headlong into my grand visions more often than not. It turned out it wasn't so easy to simultaneously feed my fragile artistic ego, keep impostor syndrome in check, an erased brand guidelines manual, and a bunch of meaningless wireframes that were the opposite of innovative design.

Meanwhile, the client's creative brief usually looked something like this: "It needs to be stylish, fresh, and modern. Ideally so cool that people want to lick it. But at the same time, it should feel familiar and all existing standards and conventions must be followed. And please don't use blue because our competitors do, or green because our CEO doesn't like green. And no images, and fonts shouldn't cost anything. And just use Lorem ipsum for now because our copy isn't ready and we don't want to use the old texts."

No wonder so many Art Directors become bitter and hate themselves as much as they hate their clients. ;-)

Or you do what I did. You cross over to the light side. You leave coolness behind (it's just a construct anyway) and step into the logical, rational world of UX. No more clients who think Gotham and Arial look basically the same so we might as well use Arial.

Suddenly my design (yes, I've now started calling wireframes design too) isn't questioned at every single point. There are relevant questions about flows, customer value, and usability. It's like we've found a common language and are striving toward the same goal. My arguments are backed up by facts, tests, interviews, analytics, and can't be shot down as arbitrarily as the ducks in Duck Hunt.

So what? Should everyone become a UX designer and we end up with a digital world consisting of gray boxes, arrows, and sticky notes in Miro? Absolutely not. What's required is

The insight that UX and UI are two sides of the same coin. They're different, sometimes you only see one, sometimes only the other, but they're connected and can't be separated. With a shared language, it suddenly becomes easier to discuss the visual part, how it connects to UX, contributes to a better experience, and most importantly, tie it to evidence based on facts, research, and testing.

Leave coolness for those who still doubt themselves and their design and cling to it as their only life raft. You don't need it anymore. With your new superpowers, you can fly.

title: Många goda råd som jag själv inte följt.
text:
# Many Good Pieces of Advice That I Myself Haven't Followed

Created on 2020-12-01 10:23
Published on 2020-12-01 10:48

I only managed three weeks of regular blogging. There were fist pumps, high fives, and cheers. And what happens now? I stumble and can't deliver. Sure, it's no big deal, the sun still rises and nobody has really noticed that I haven't posted anything. It's hardly as serious as skipping rent or being late with the kids' pocket money. That's where you can talk about consequences.

But for me it felt like a failure. If only I had planned better, started earlier, etc. Well, if I make some lemonade out of it, at least I got a good idea for my delayed post.

Creating new positive habits is difficult.

An important rule (which I absolutely haven't followed) is to start simple. Something so simple that you can't fail at it. Instead of doing ten push-ups, which can be tough if you're untrained, start with one. Pretty much anyone can do one a day. Then you build slowly and gradually from there.

As for me, from now on I'm setting aside a quarter hour a day to write. I can manage that. I won't finish an entire article in a quarter hour, but after a couple of quarters it should be ready. To make it even easier, I'll set an alarm for the exact same time every day.

Next tip (which I haven't followed either).

Expect that you're going to stumble. It will happen sooner or later so don't be caught off guard. For some reason we (or at least I) always plan from an optimal scenario. When I estimate how long a job will take, I only think as the crow flies. How long does it take from A to B. I don't account for the fact that I don't know the way, where the destination is, uncertainty factors, miscalculations, the mental work, all the mistakes needed to find the right path. The reason is that we go by previous end results that seem so very simple and obvious. All the iterations, discussions, and detours that were needed to get there have already been forgotten.

For my part, I need to account for the fact that it takes longer than I think. I need to create strategies for how I'll act if things don't go as planned. If I miss posting an entry one week, it's not a failure, it's just a bump in the road. The most important thing is to come back and make sure to write at least one post the week after.

The final tip (yes, you get it).

Set clear sub-goals. "Starting to write" is a very abstract goal. You never really finish, it just keeps going and going. If you want to learn an instrument, a good sub-goal might be to learn a simple song by heart. It becomes concrete right away, you know when you're done, and then you just set the next sub-goal.

My goal is to write 20 posts. I can do that. Then we'll see. ;-)

Bonus tip 1.

Reward yourself with a reward after each completed sub-goal. A piece of chocolate, a cup of latte, or 15 minutes of video games. Suddenly it feels a lot more fun even if you stumbled at the finish line.

Bonus tip 2

Listen to the podcast Monkey Mindset for more tips on mental training and personal development. https://www.monkeymindset.se/

title: Män är från Mars, kvinnorna från Venus och kunderna från helvetet.
text:
# Men are from Mars, Women are from Venus and Customers are from Hell.

Created on 2020-12-07 16:36
Published on 2020-12-07 17:02

It's almost become a sport for designers to express anger and frustration over their work not receiving the appreciation it deserves. The customers who are never satisfied, come with endless revisions and slowly but surely transform a perfect design into a train wreck. "Can't you just make the logo bigger. Just liiiitle, and liiiitle more."

If it's not the customer causing trouble, it's the boss, the project manager, or someone else who wants to "add" some fancy design to a random project.

This frustration has taken shape in countless memes, forum complaints, songs, YouTube videos and even this entertaining collection at
https://clientsfromhell.net/

Most stories create strong recognition and make you both laugh and cry, often simultaneously. I've spent late nights myself making changes I happened to promise and now paid dearly for.

What's common to all these scenarios is that they're always told from the designer's perspective. The customer is the antagonist who, due to malice, ignorance or sheer stupidity, causes problems for our design hero. The customer doesn't even get to give their version of events, just bear the responsibility for the entire situation. This is how you build trenches, walls and strengthen the "us-versus-them" mentality.

It can definitely be fun reading with many bad situations and misunderstandings. And now comes the one. Wait for it…..

BUT….

This isn't a solution. Not even a good problem statement. It's just a symptom of poor communication. It's surely obvious that your customer can't know as much about design as you do, that they can't tell the difference between Futura and Helvetica, or that they want their logo to be visible. If they knew as much as you, they would've done it themselves and saved themselves the trouble and money.

You need to work with what your customer has and what they know. They know their business and they have a vision where your design serves a purpose. To succeed with your assignment, you need to understand both your customer's current situation and what they want to achieve with your help. You need to create a common language, a babelfish that translates your customer's business goals into design.

Does the customer say the logo should be bigger? Perfect. Let them explain what they expect a bigger logo to accomplish. Maybe it should make it very clear which page the visitor has landed on, create brand awareness, showcase their new logo, etc. Is there another solution, like clever copy that both mentions the brand and explains the customer benefit? Or a compromise where the logo is large on the landing page but not elsewhere on the site?

The moment the designer starts actively listening and involves the customer in the design process, synergy is created. It's no longer about orders and delivery, it becomes collaboration and co-creation. The customer will feel seen, understood and most importantly, get what they want and need.

You don't have to agree with everything your customer says. Customers are wrong, gladly and often. The key is communicating it in a smart way without patronizing them. Or worst of all, doing exactly what they ask and then taking the blame for poor results. Instead, suggest alternative solutions with the same goal. Or why not test it—for example, an A/B test pits your customer's idea and your design against each other. If the customer wins, they get to feel smart; if you win, you have objective arguments for your case.

Next time you think your customer is really bad at understanding design, you might ask yourself how good you are at communicating. It's not the customer's job to understand design, but it's your job to explain it.

I've innovated my blog. Instead of writing myself, I've brought in three heavyweights in innovation and asked them some really tough and interesting questions. That way I avoid doing a bunch of research but can still bask in the glow of their expertise. Innovation for the WIN. #future #innovation #segway #googleglass

title: På tal om innovation
text:
# Speaking of Innovation

Speaking of Innovation
Created on 2020-12-15 16:39
Published on 2020-12-15 17:10

All companies want to innovate. Or rather, they have to innovate. The pace of development is constantly increasing, new hardware, new software, constant updates, new customer groups with new needs, demands and expectations. But how do you innovate in a good way? Most established companies have their niche, their USPs and their sacred cows—or rather golden cows they want to keep milking to recoup their previous investments. How much should you invest in new ideas that might turn out to be dead ends?

Innovation is difficult. Not just the act of innovating, but innovating in the right amount. Because innovative products that are ahead of their time feel foreign or even frightening to consumers. Segway and Google Glass were very innovative and ahead of both their time and their competitors. But that was also what meant that neither demand, recognition, nor the social codes were in place. Imagine coming whizzing along on your ridiculous Segway with a pair of shiny new Googles on your nose. Would you be seen as a cool innovator or a nerdy wannabe? Can you wear your Googles when sitting and having coffee with a friend without seeming pretentious? All this uncertainty means that the general public doesn't dare buy a completely unknown product. It remains an odd gadget among *innovators* and *early adopters*.

So the trick is to stay ahead of the curve without falling off the edge. How do you do it? I have no idea. (Just because I'm writing this article doesn't mean I have all the answers.) So I ask some people who do know. I welcome *Peter Sandberg*, who works with innovation at Rise and runs the podcast Spinnovation, *Johan Berndtsson*, founder of inUse and now a freelance consultant in innovation and management, and *Maria Idebro*, management consultant at THREAD.

**What's the most common question you get about innovation?**

**Peter:**
I rarely get the same question over and over, but many of my guests on the Spinnovation podcast have emphasized the importance of innovation having to come from the top. Without leadership, and even the board of directors, being involved in enabling innovation and backing the innovation work at every level, it becomes very difficult to achieve change.

**Maria:**
A common question is whether it's smartest/most efficient to have innovation separate or integrated. That is, whether you should have, for example, an innovation lab working on the future on the side, or whether it's something everyone should work with in some form.

**Johan:**
It depends on who's asking. If it's people further down in the organization, it's often about how to draw attention to the challenges they see. They're looking for a way to make leaders feel a sense of urgency. If the question comes from management level, it's usually about how to work together with customers to identify new opportunities for the business.

**Has the view of innovation changed recently, especially during 2020?**

**Peter:**
I don't think the view of innovation has changed that much. However, many organizations have been forced to become more innovative because of Corona. Perhaps not so much in what they offer, but in the way they work. All crises contribute to increased innovation. Another thing is that a standard for innovation management has actually been introduced and you can now become certified as an innovation leader. A sign that innovation is being taken more seriously and that innovation management is moving from being a role to becoming a profession.

**Maria:**
2020 has of course "forced" many to innovate in different ways. What I think might be lasting is that we've gotten such a clear example of how the world can change quickly. Overnight, the behaviors of customers, suppliers, or collaborators can change. It becomes clear that you need to be able to build the capacity to pivot quickly.

**Johan:**
Since I left inUse at the end of 2019, I've worked a lot with online grocery retail. A very interesting industry, not least because the high picking and distribution costs eat up the initially low margins. When the coronavirus then caused demand to skyrocket, it suddenly became central to find offerings that add more value to the customer, and which they're therefore willing to pay more for. What's required for this is processes that make it possible to work research-driven and results-oriented.

**With so many ongoing trends and rapid development, how do you know which direction to innovate in?**

**Peter:**
You can never know for sure. It can be useful to compare chess and poker. Chess is complicated but poker is complex. The difference lies in the availability of information. In chess, both players, and any spectators, have access to all information. There is no hidden information. In poker, there's a lot of information that isn't known, such as what cards others are holding, which have been discarded, and which remain in the deck. This lack of information makes poker complex. The same analogy applies to companies and innovation. Building a jumbo jet isn't complex for a company like Boeing, it's "just" complicated because they have all the information and knowledge needed. Building a new product or service is complex because there's so much unknown information you don't yet have about, for example, the market, customer needs, technologies, etc. You can never be completely certain you're doing the right thing. The solution in that case is to have a good innovation process to rely on that enables rapid experimentation and enables rapid hypothesis testing.

**Maria:**
Combine collecting data with actively deciding which direction you want to innovate in. 1. Here's what we know. 2. What do we want? What company do we want to be? And then work agile so you continuously keep learning and experimenting.

**Johan:**
My impression is that companies, employees, and management have pretty good ideas rooted in what's going on in their own industry. If you supplement these with qualitative and quantitative studies of customer behavior, and with analyses of larger trends, you have a fairly good picture to start from. When you've then shaped a hypothesis about where you want to be, it's "just" a matter of testing your way forward in small steps. But... That's where it usually falls apart. In the execution. Sometimes because there are unclear roles and responsibilities, but usually because there's a lack of working methods, processes, and routines when it comes to testing in small steps. Pilot studies that could be completed in days or weeks turn into projects that take months, or years, to carry out.

**Do you have to innovate yourself? Can't you just ride on other people's innovations that prove to work?**

**Peter:**
Yes, you can build very successful companies based on other people's innovations or even just by copying successful concepts. We see examples of this everywhere, for instance consulting firms, staffing companies, and gaming sites. However, that's not innovation because nothing new has been created that didn't exist before.

**Maria:**
You probably need to have some innovation power anyway so you're not last on the ball if conditions change. But not everyone maybe needs to be super innovative. A good trick can be to ride the wake of someone who's ahead. Oh, you're going to Mars? Maybe we could be the ones who livestream the landing. Or come up with a solution so you can eat popcorn in zero gravity.

**Johan:**
It depends on how large the market you're operating in is, and how late you come to the table. Often it works fine to just ride along with others, but if it's an industry and market where many players are crowded together, it becomes very difficult to just copy. Then you need to find some other value, like a truly unique brand, but in this context that's not much of a moat, and the risk is that you fall back when customers decide that someone else is trendier.

**How do you tell the difference between good and bad innovation / ways of innovating?**

**Peter:**
Almost by definition, you could say that bad innovation is something that isn't used or gets no demand. A bad way to innovate is to develop something that's tech-driven but doesn't solve a concrete problem or need.

**Maria:**
Maybe I'm not answering exactly the question, but I think values will play an increasingly important role going forward. A bad innovation process is one where you lose sight of your values and only steer by other things.

**Johan:**
Bad innovation goes unused. Money down the drain for whoever invested, and for society and the environment a gadget or service that consumed resources and takes up space unnecessarily. Bad ways to innovate... There are many, but maybe the ethical perspective is the most important to highlight here. I'm a big fan of agile working methods and testing your way forward, but you have to be careful so it doesn't happen at people's expense. "Move fast and break things," or "fail fast," is not okay if it involves, for example, people's feelings, relationships, money, or health. On a small scale, you can cause extreme stress. An encouragement on Facebook to celebrate your dead daughter's 6th birthday.... A bank account that suddenly stands at zero, the same day the bills are due.... On a larger scale, you cause suicide; on an even larger scale, you can cause genocide, as Zuckerberg and Facebook have shown. Innovation requires care.

**Should you invest in an R&D department or should the whole company innovate?**

**Peter:**
Many companies have cut back on their own R&D to instead invest in open innovation, which means going outside the organization to find external solutions and solution providers. In some companies, innovation is driven more by the marketing department, which through their close contact with customers are often the first to pick up on new needs. All employees can and should be involved in innovation work, but it also places demands on management, culture, and organization for it to work.

**Maria:**
Ha! I took that as an answer to a question at the beginning! :) I think you should innovate in a decentralized way as much as possible. But maybe a combination.

**Johan:**
Yes. To be successful, you need to have some centralized capacity of your own, but you also need to involve the whole company. There's simply so much valuable knowledge there, and it would be a waste not to use it. Finding simple ways to engage the entire company is also a good way to build culture, energy, and cohesion. Beyond this, you need to work with both partners and customers. Partners because you rarely have all the competence needed to create a complete solution, especially today when innovation is so much about building ecosystems of services where you rarely have full control over everything. Customers must be involved because, in the final analysis, they're the ones who will buy and use the services that are developed.

**How much of your time or budget should you invest in innovation?**

**Peter:**
There's no exact answer, but I think 5% is a reasonable minimum level.

**Johan:**
I don't have a good answer here... So I'll ask a counter-question instead. If a software developer where 50% of the workforce continuously works on developing and refining the service they offer—are they working with innovation? Or is it only the 2% working on "future projects" that innovate? ;)

**Is there anything else I've forgotten to ask or that you'd like to add?**

**Peter:**
Jack Welch, the legendary CEO of GE, said decades ago that "When the pace of change on the outside is greater than the pace of change on the inside, then you can begin to sense the end." I don't think anyone thinks the pace of change is lower today than it was then. Therefore, innovation has never been more important.

Thank you for your wise answers.

For 16 weeks I've deceived a large group of people without being caught. They were even happy and grateful. Best of all, I even got paid for the trouble.

That's how it felt this week when I finished the course I taught at Nackademin. A mixture of pride, relief and amazement. Completely unbelievable that it worked out, without anyone exposing (or even suspecting) that I was just pretending to be a teacher, that I actually don't know what I'm talking about, that I lack relevant education, solid experience, haven't done any of my own research or contributed any original insights. That no one noticed I just cobbled together some stuff I found on the internet and threw it together in a PowerPoint. No one stood up and said "the teacher has no clothes." (At least I'm wearing underwear below the waist when I'm zooming).

But all the impostor syndrome disappeared once I got to see all the final presentations and realized they were incredibly impressive. The students didn't just listen and understand what I was trying to tell them in my rambling, but they also applied everything correctly, found new insights, dared to scrap ideas that didn't work, tested, brainstormed, reasoned, sketched, prototyped and came up with sleek, innovative concepts.

Then I felt like a proud parent who watched their children graduate. Imagine how fast they grow.

I'm really glad I managed to ignore that voice that said "who do you think you are, you don't know anything about design thinking or user-centered design." Because even though that voice was partly right (I know significantly more about both now than before the course), it was wrong on one important point. I AM exactly the right person for the job. Just watch me.

title: Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal
text:
Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal
Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal
Created on 2020-12-23 10:20
Published on 2020-12-23 10:50

There is no stronger tradition than Christmas. It is the most Swedish thing we have (apart from Walpurgis Night and Midsummer). Many want to experience the magical feeling of Christmas that they enjoyed as children and pass that gift on to their own children. Everything else can change as it wishes, but no one bloody well touches Christmas. It is in these rituals and traditions that we create our identity and a stable point in this chaotic existence. No wonder Christmas 2020 seems more Christmas-like than ever, despite the absence of snow and Christmas crowds. When we can't or aren't allowed to meet physically, people are sending Christmas cards and packages like never before, and all food delivery services have been fully booked for ages. We'll show that fucking corona how Christmas is supposed to be celebrated.

At the same time, the year 2020 shows us that normal is just a construction. Everything we are expected to be, how we should act, what is considered proper or improper—these are not absolute values or truths. It's an agreement we have voluntarily or involuntarily signed with those around us. In other words, just someone else's idea that you've adopted as your own. What was considered normal yesterday is seen as completely insane today. Like beating your child with a stick (preferably in public), playing professional hockey without a helmet, offering a pregnant woman a cigarette, or hugging a friend in a packed restaurant.

Sometimes these changes happen infinitely slowly (as with Christmas traditions), other times painfully fast (hello corona). But if this year has shown us anything, it's that we can change when we really want to or when we really have to. If we really want to, we can stop burning rainforests, stop letting greed and profit margins dictate, stop destroying the planet, and instead be kinder, more generous, and let others win without us having to lose. We can even continue celebrating Christmas even though it will never be the way it was before.

We just need to let go of the rails and stop gazing longingly at the time before the pandemic. That time is over. Our job now is to create the new normal for next year. A new reality that is much better than the normal of 2020 and before.

Have a truly Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal 2021.

title: Det stora penistestet som varje designer bör göra.
text:
# The Big Penis Test Every Designer Should Do

Created on 2021-01-27 10:19
Published on 2021-01-27 13:34

Let's face it. We humans have dirty minds. We react to anything that alludes to sex and even when there's nothing there, we're happy to create an association. Elongated things become penises, round and curved objects transform into breasts and buttocks 🍆 🍑. This has been used in art and advertising since time immemorial. From the sublime to the playful (like the example below), to the provocative to the vulgar or tasteless (unfortunately far too many examples). Our (dirty) imagination doesn't need much to get going.

The tricky part is when you do it unintentionally. Especially when it comes to logos, symbols or icons where there's room for interpretation. As a designer, you're always trying to find a new angle, make the logo unique and exciting. You change, tweak, rotate, add and subtract. You're so deep in your design that you become blind to the obvious. That's when, my friend, it's time for the Penis Test. You don't need a ruler for it, you don't even need to have a penis. Inclusive and free from performance requirements.

Start with a simple (but oh so important) question. Does your logo look like a penis? (or something else that's equivocal?)

Answer: No.

Great, you can continue the test.

Answer: Yes.

Is it supposed to look like or allude to a penis? If so, you've succeeded, your logo is done. If it's not supposed to look like a penis but does anyway, it's best to scrap the proposal and start over.

Explore all angles.

Your logo will be seen from all sides and corners. Make sure it holds up when rotated, inverted or turned into a pattern. Office of Government Commerce (OGC) becomes something completely different unintentionally when rotated.

Negative space.

You're not just designing the shape itself but also the spaces between. If you're clever and skillful, you can exploit this, as in the classic FedEx logo (the arrow between the E and x). Otherwise, it can have unexpected consequences.

Typography.

Choice of typeface, spacing between letters and words affects not only readability but also word shape. Innocent words suddenly take on a completely different meaning. Even pairing lowercase c, d and b together can create a problematic word image. A lowercase l can easily become a capital I. No harm done if you're not simultaneously turning a letter into a heart. (Thanks Locum for this unforgettable and world-famous example.)

Figures.

Is the logo supposed to represent a person or a creature? Make sure the figure doesn't have any objects near its crotch. Should multiple figures interact? Examine carefully that this interaction cannot be misinterpreted. Haven't you designed a figure but it appears anyway? It could be a fun Easter egg as long as the figure isn't doing anything inappropriate, as was the case with A style clothing.

Names.

It's almost an unwritten rule that no matter what word you choose, it means something obscene in some language or slang. With today's digital presence, the chance or risk is high that people from other countries will come into contact with your product. Google is your best friend here.

Good luck. May the schwartz be with you.

After last week's giggle, it's a bit more serious this time. This is a subject I've wrestled with for a long time and only in recent years has it started to really sink in. I'm talking, of course, about receiving and handling negative criticism.

title: Hantera negativ kritik
text:
# Handle Negative Criticism

It's so easy to receive positive feedback. Then it's called feedback or praise, not criticism. You get an ego boost and finally some recognition for all the work you've put in. However, receiving the emotional slap in the face that negative criticism entails is not as easy. The very concept of "receiving criticism" or criticizing has a strong negative connotation. It becomes a discomfort that you immediately want to swat away. I've handled it in three bad ways and one good way. I'll take the bad ones first:

## React with anger

The first impulse, which unfortunately I've followed far too many times, is to see the criticism (and the critic) as a threat that must be fought. I wanted to immediately show that the person was wrong and threw out every conceivable argument to prove it. At best, they're factual and structured, at worst they're personal attacks like "but what about you..." The consequence is a fight or at least bad feelings, a damaged relationship, and nothing gets resolved.

## Play dead

The second way to handle criticism is to do the exact opposite. I lie down flat, admit everything, and bend to the criticism. I become small, almost invisible, and just let the dark cloud pass. If I just don't argue back, nothing bad will happen to me. I don't want a confrontation or to seem difficult. This was a common scenario early in my career when a more senior person criticized my design. I lacked the security and self-confidence to see the criticism as a suggestion for improvement rather than a list of flaws that needed fixing. The consequence is that I might change something that was actually good, that I don't stand up for my work, and that my confidence sinks even further.

## Hear no evil

The third technique that I want to advise against is to ignore it. Maybe you pretend to listen, nod, say that you understand, and then do what you want anyway. My children have a black belt in this noble art. On the surface, it seems like a constructive dialogue is taking place, you might even discuss in detail what changes need to be made and why. Then I (the critic) walk away satisfied with my contribution and hopeful for a result that never materializes. Consequence. This method is tricky because everything seems peaceful and pleasant. But as soon as the bluff is exposed, conflict arises, and if I've been hiding something, I now need to come up with a bunch of excuses and explanations.

All three methods have something in common. I, as the recipient of the criticism, learn nothing, avoid no mistakes, and don't grow as a person. There's no good dialogue or synergy with the other party. Especially when it comes to design, where both the result and I as a designer benefit from constructive criticism.

So what now? Is there a good way to do it? I'm glad you asked. This is a way that works for me.

## Disconnect the emotions.

Since (negative) criticism often triggers strong emotions, it's important to keep them under control. A good trick is to imagine that the problem has befallen a friend who is now asking for advice. Then it's much easier to disconnect your ego and the need to defend it.

## Accept, ask, and reflect.

Don't do anything hasty. Don't answer immediately even if the matter is urgent. If the criticism is written in an email, read it several times and try to understand what the other party is after. If you receive the criticism in a conversation, buy time by asking follow-up questions and asking for time to think before you can give an answer. Note that you have neither agreed with nor rejected the criticism. You have only received it as a package with unknown contents that you will unpack calmly and carefully.

## Analyze and structure.

By pure instinct, you start looking for evidence for your own case and flaws in the other person's argument. But could there be something to what the other person is saying? What is the reason for the criticism and what does the other person want to achieve by bringing it up? Is the criticism concrete and factual or is it based on opinions and assumptions? Is it directed at a thing, you as a person, or your behavior? What do you agree with, what do you disagree with, and what lies somewhere in between? It's not about deciding who is right or who won.

## The way forward.

Criticism is a story that needs a resolution. Even if you don't intend to make any changes to your original plan, it's important to communicate why. Maybe there wasn't time for such major changes, or other factors that would make it not work. The main thing is that you show you've taken the criticism to heart, thought it over, and made a decision based on it. The critic often wants a dialogue, not for everything to be exactly as they thought. Get the other person to feel heard and half the problem is solved.

This may sound simple but it's incredibly difficult. Especially when our mind is made to avoid discomfort, where one negative thing weighs more than ten positive ones. So my last piece of advice is to be proactive. Assume that you could be wrong, ask for feedback, and create a dialogue. Suddenly, criticism doesn't feel like a stinging slap in the face but rather a helping hand. Good luck.

Everyone talks about the importance of feedback, yet few companies have a good feedback culture. How come and why should it be so difficult? "

title: Nyckeln till bra feedback-kultur
text:
# The Key to Good Feedback Culture

During my time in school, feedback was part of everyday life. Particularly at design school, it was a given that your work would be examined and questioned. It was painful to see many late nights of hard work being questioned and picked apart. I had a strong urge to throw everything into a burning waste basket. After a few rounds, I managed to get over the negative feelings and realized that the design actually got better after a tough critique. My critical thinking improved and I found many flaws before the design was put to the test. I could no longer gloss over a bad idea with some flashy design and a cool typeface.

Judge my surprise when I realized that in working life, good feedback is a rare commodity. All reflection was saved for the annual development conversation with virtually no follow-up. As employees (designers), we're expected to be creative, innovative, high-performing and productive. We should think outside the box, learn new things and skills, but without making any (costly) mistakes. All this without receiving continuous, constructive feedback.

Why is this feedback thing so difficult? Both giving it, receiving it, asking for it and offering it. Is it the feeling of being criticized, having your competence questioned, or the fear of being perceived as a pessimistic complainer? Many prefer to drive a project into the ditch convinced they're right rather than have their opinions questioned in time.

So plant a seed

How do you, as an individual, reverse a feedback-skeptical organization? The best way is to start gently and ask for feedback yourself. Don't use the word criticism, not even constructive criticism. Even the word feedback can be very loaded and get people to slowly back out of the room. Package the feedback session as improvement suggestions, sounding board, problem-finding, iteration, or some other empty buzzword. Say that you've gotten stuck with an idea, sketch, or prototype and need help moving forward. It's good to have a few concrete questions up your sleeve to steer the conversation in the right direction. For example, "How can I make the design feel more educational?" or "Is there anything in the design that feels unclear to you?"

Repeat this procedure until it feels completely natural for both you and your colleagues. Don't forget to thank people for feedback even if you disagree or don't plan to make the suggested changes. Hopefully you've planted a seed for a thriving feedback culture. Now you can carefully offer your help when your colleagues get stuck (in other words, need feedback).

Prerequisites for a constructive feedback culture

Framework:
Set the right level for how you want feedback to function. How and when you give it, what it should contain, what tone it needs to have, whether there's a moderator, and so on. With a framework in place, you don't need to find it tricky or awkward—you just follow the protocol. It's good to have a battery of the most common questions so it's easy to get the session started.

Safety:
It can be very difficult to receive feedback even if it's constructive and well-formulated. So I, as the receiver, need to feel safe and know that the feedback is honest but comes with all good intentions and the goal of helping. Time and place are also important—preferably a one-on-one or in a closed setting where what's said doesn't get repeated elsewhere.

Trust:
Who gives the feedback is at least as important as what is said. Is it some guy on Flashback or is it your closest friend? You can still listen to both, but the probability is higher that you'll take feedback to heart from someone you trust, look up to and respect.

Routine:
Feedback is like exercise. It gets easier and better the more often you do it. Short and frequent is better than long and rare. Get feedback as a natural part of every partial delivery, check-in or presentation, and it becomes easier to see the positive effects of feedback.

What feedback culture exists at your company or organization? Is there anyone actively working on this question?

title: Världens kortaste inlägg
text:
# The World's Shortest Post

Created on 2021-04-21 07:34
Published on 2021-04-21 07:50

How's the blogging going anyway? asks my guilty conscience a bit testily.

So-so, I answer. I knew exactly what I wanted to write. A good, well-formulated, researched post about feedback.

So when it didn't happen, nothing happened at all? No plan B?

That's right, I answer. All or nothing.

I keep falling into the same holes I warn others about. There's no reason for me to read all these books about habits and behavior if I'm not going to translate it into action. Knowledge that doesn't affect behavior is just information.

Which is precisely why this post will be extra short. So that it actually gets done. Proof in itself that it's better to do something small often than something big very rarely. The consequences of missing a short daily session are much less than missing the week's bootcamp.

Doing something simple and often is a perfect cure for perfectionism, performance anxiety, and writer's block. Focus on frequency and quantity instead of grand gestures and quality, and all demands and expectations disappear. The magic is that you get quality thrown in for free. You become good, sort of by stealth, without having to kill yourself in the process.

Try it yourself. Spend 15 minutes a day on something new, like learning Spanish on Duolingo like I do. You'll be surprised how much you've learned after two months. Let me know how it goes. Hasta la vista.
Words can be completely insignificant but also make a very big difference. Replace one word with a similar one and the outcome becomes something entirely different.

title: Små ord som skapa stor skillnad
text:
# Small Words That Make a Big Difference

One of the most important phases at the beginning of a project is defining the problem, setting clear goals, and writing a "why." Nothing complicated—if you want a successful project, everyone needs to know what they should do and why. But what people often don't think about is how much impact word choice and wording have. Imagine we're starting an important project that will get our company or product back on track. Here are different ways to describe the initiative:

We're going to crush our competitors and seize their customers.
We're going to outrun our competitors and steal their customers.
We're going to outshine our competitors and attract their customers to us.

Do we call our meeting room a war room, planning room, inspiration room, or play room? Should we prevent users from making mistakes or should we make it easier for them to do the right thing?

It might seem trivial to weigh every word when they mean roughly the same thing. Everyone understands anyway... But it's not about understanding the meaning. Same basic meaning but different tonality sends thoughts in completely different directions and also affects which solutions we choose. Design work doesn't start in Sketch, it starts with choosing the right words.

It's important to find a tone that fits you, your culture, and your users. If you're a bunch of hungry stockbrokers, maybe you should go out and crush all resistance and own your opponents. But this vocabulary probably won't work as well for a group of preschool teachers.

Is your day filled with a bunch of musts? Try replacing the word "must" with something else.

"I get to fold the laundry, I have the honor of picking up the children, it's going to be inspiring to go grocery shopping, I'm looking forward to cleaning out the garage."

I know, it's a bit silly. You still have to do the boring stuff. But just like the exercise "yes and..." instead of "yes but..." a positively charged word creates a completely different energy and momentum. Try it yourself.

More on "Yes but, yes and..." https://www.metodbanken.se/post/ja-men-ja-och

title: Still faking it
text:
# Still faking it

Still faking it
Created on 2021-05-24 15:55
Published on 2021-05-24 16:21

I've always had trouble with the phrase "Fake it til you make it". It felt so inauthentic. So fake ;-) Why should you pretend to be something you're not? Surely it's better to get good at something first and then proudly say you can do it, instead of just pretending.

In many cases, that's true. I don't want to be operated on by a doctor who's still learning from their mistakes, or sit on a plane with a pilot who's testing things out. But for many other, less critical situations, it's the fastest and sometimes the only way forward. Take, for example, the eternal chicken-or-egg dilemma: you can't get a job without experience and you can't get experience without a job. If you don't have the luck of a very lenient employer, you need to bluff your way into your first job. "Yeah, I've done loads of that before, no problem." Then rush home, ninja-Google what they wanted you to be able to do, and learn it in record time while having anxiety the first month. But then you're in the matrix.

Faking is a psychological mechanism you can exploit as a life hack. Imagine you want to start jogging regularly. Right now you're an ordinary, untrained person who wants to get in shape. You also think and behave like an ordinary untrained person who wants to get in shape. All your decisions are made from that perspective.

But what if you pretended to be what you want to become? By faking your new identity as a jogger (which you absolutely aren't yet), you can influence your thought patterns and behavior. To the question "Should I go out jogging?", your old ordinary self would answer "nah, it's raining, better do it tomorrow. I'm also a bit tired from work." But you are now your new "self", the jogger, who answers "how nice a bit of rain" and happily puts on your running shoes and a rain jacket.

Want to quit smoking? Answer "No thanks, I don't smoke" next time you're offered a cigarette. And it's true, your new "self" doesn't smoke. Now it's just about your old "self" catching up.

The biggest fake of all must be parenthood. Every day you have to play someone who's got it together, who remembers packed lunches, parent-teacher meetings and buying new wellington boots. Someone who sets sensible, fair and consistent rules, who's always there and solves every problem and crisis. Maybe there are a few superhumans who actually have it figured out. The rest of us just take life as it comes and fake it. Every child is really a massive social experiment and most of the time it works out pretty well.

So keep on faking, you will make it.

Here's my experience anyway, from UX to product, from consultant to in-house. #productdesign #ui #ux #learnifier #designer #product

title: Vad gör en produktdesigner hela dagarna?
text:
# What Does a Product Designer Do All Day?

I was very nervous when, after eight years as a UX/UI consultant, I switched to working in-house as a product designer. My biggest fear was that I would get bored. As a consultant, I got to work with many clients, with great variation in both size, industry, level of detail, and project duration. There was always a rush to diving into a new assignment, almost like a superhero coming to save the day.

Suddenly working with the same product, day in and day out, seemed like a nightmare. How much can you really optimize a checkout flow anyway?

My second worry was that I didn't actually know what a product designer did. Quite a bit of imposter syndrome on that front. On paper, the jobs looked similar—create prototypes, test, talk to developers. Why was it called something different then?

The first point was answered almost immediately. There were so many different features to develop and test that I would hardly have time to get bored. The only thing that stayed the same was the visual identity. That also needed to be reviewed, but that was a separate project.

The second point took a bit longer to figure out. It wasn't about processes, tools, or deliverables. It was almost entirely about attitude. A kind of mental hierarchy. Just as UI is part of UX, I saw how UX became part of product design. Besides caring for users' needs and experience, I also had to ensure the product grew, developed in the right direction, and continued to generate revenue. It wasn't a quick consultant gig, a hit-and-run. I would actually get to enjoy the results of all the hard work, see how things I'd designed were received by users. I'd get the chance to fix any mistakes and work on new iterations. At the same time, I'd have to live with all my previous decisions, so it was best to make sure they were as good as possible or could be changed later.

Now I'm in my second job as a product designer and I enjoy it more than ever. I was very careful to choose a product I believe in, a company I enjoy working at, and an industry that creates value and isn't just chasing money. That part is important. Think of it as a relationship. It's you and the product—you should make each other happy for many years to come, stand by each other through both downturns and victories.

So to answer the question in the headline: What do I actually do? I create flows in Miro, lead workshops, interview customers, collect feedback and wishes from customer support, stay in close contact with developers, prototype new features, do visual design, and right now I'm laying the foundation for an entirely new design system and product redesign. I've probably forgotten something…

Are you a UI and/or UX designer who wants to take the next step and find new challenges? Do you want to learn more about the business side of design? Then product designer might be a good choice. Small difference on the outside but big on the inside. You'll grow as a designer and broaden your horizons.

Besides, I have a secret to tell you. Just for you who've read this far, my dear, loyal reader. If you seriously want to give product design a shot and become my wingman, there's a link at the bottom. We'll be like Han and Chewie, Doc and Marty, or Emil and Alfred. We have exciting times ahead: creating a new design system, building a design organization, connecting design with analytics, and taking a growing product to new heights. We'll give each other honest feedback, support, praise, inspire, and push each other outside our comfort zones. You could be an old UX fox or a rising star. I'm already looking forward to your application. See you there.

https://career.learnifier.com/jobs/1160301-product-ux-designer

title: Det finns ingen hemlig hemlig sås
text:
# There is no secret sauce

Created on 2021-09-02 08:54
Published on 2021-09-02 09:11

As a child, I looked up to my parents. They knew everything and had answers to all my questions. The teachers at school were the same way—they possessed all the knowledge, which they then generously scattered over the ignorant masses of minions. "One day I'm going to be like them and know everything," I thought.

Guess what? That day never came. I grew up, but the feeling of having all the answers never arrived. On the contrary, it got even worse when I had my first child. I didn't know a damn thing about how to raise a kid. I wasn't a superhuman. I had no answers about the meaning of life or what to do when you grow up. I had to wing it from one day to the next. My child became both a test subject and the answer key. What worked brilliantly one day was completely ineffective the next.

But I did become good at one thing: keeping panic at bay. When I didn't have control over the situation, I had to pretend I knew what I was doing. "We're not lost at all. We're just taking an unexplored detour."

You just have to accept it: all parents throughout history have just been faking it. They had no control or plan. They simply closed their eyes and went for it, doing exactly what their parents did, or the complete opposite, hoping that their way was the right one. There is no secret sauce, just like in Kung Fu Panda. No Morpheus to give you the red pill and reveal reality. You're stuck in the Matrix and you know it.

What does this have to do with design?, you might ask. Quite a lot, actually. I realized the same pattern existed at work too. As a junior designer, I looked up to my older, more experienced colleagues with fancy titles and high salaries. Like a little chick gaping for food, I craved answers to my questions and the magic formula to banish my constant insecurity.

But guess what? Your senior colleagues are faking it too. They don't have absolute answers or any real grasp of the situation. At best, they have educated guesses based on previous experience. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. Same with big established companies. You might think a prestigious bank has a better grip on reality than a startup. But hardly. They're just as confused, they take wild shots and mess up constantly, regardless of how many layers of middle management they've managed to create.

This sounds like complaining and bad news. But actually, it's a good thing. You no longer have to wait to be good enough, to know enough, or for a stone tablet with all the answers to come sailing down from heaven. Nobody has a clue. An expert is right just as often (or as rarely) as a layperson. If someone succeeded, they either got lucky or failed a hundred times before finally getting it right.

The best way to gain more control is to accept that you can never have full control and plan for every circumstance. The only way forward is to just go for it and take things as they come. "Roll with the punches," as they say in English.

Have you ever wondered how a digital product actually comes into being? Here you might get the answer.
