title: Vad gör en Product Manager hela dagarna?
text:
# What Does a Product Manager Do All Day?

Created on 2021-10-06 15:13
Published on 2021-10-07 09:02

If you work with any kind of digital product, a Product Manager is someone you really need to understand. They're the one who charts the future of the product and, by extension, your future too. But how does a PM know where to go and how to get there? I'm interviewing Nina Holtz, PM at Funnel, and Viktor Löfgren, PM at WOC.

Not many people know what a Product Manager does. Do you have a good elevator pitch you usually use for outsiders?

**Nina:**
I usually say that together with developers and designers, I make sure we prioritize and build the right things in the product. I think mostly from a customer and business perspective, designers focus on the user experience, and developers on the technical solutions.

**Viktor:**
That probably depends a lot on the type of company, but it's about working with expectations. Expectations in terms of developing a product in a certain direction, understanding your customers' and users' expectations and needs, managing business and market conditions. Being the glue between users/customers and other stakeholders. I'm often the interpreter who speaks all three "languages"—technology, business, and market.

How did you become a PM? Was it luck, necessity, or a career goal?

**Nina:**
It was maybe a bit of everything. I was there when Funnel started, but I worked in the Customer Success team. In the beginning when we were so small, it was natural to work closely with the development team. I attended stand-ups and was involved in many discussions about what we should build next. As we grew, that happened less often and I missed working closely with that part of the company. Plus, I thought the PM role seemed exciting. Three years ago, I took a year's leave, and when I came back, I asked Sven, our Chief Product Officer, if I could start in his team.

**Viktor:**
It was mostly luck stemming from necessity, I'd say. I've always been very interested in the relationship between a technical solution and its users. I discovered that it was an incredible strength to be able to talk about and explain technical solutions and problems in a simple and understandable way and get people to grasp their value. From there, the step to product development wasn't far, because it worked just as well the other way around—being able to discuss customer needs with more technical teams.

Where do you make the biggest difference as a PM? In other words, if you disappeared, who or what would suffer?

**Nina:**
As a PM, I analyze data on how our customers use the product, talk to the commercial teams to understand their challenges and what they hear from customers. The context I gather from this, I try to convey to the development team so we prioritize correctly and can reach good solutions faster. I believe this context exchange would suffer if I disappeared.

**Viktor:**
If I were to remove the product manager role from my current role in a company that's really a product company, for example, I think it would take a little while before anyone noticed it was gone. But pretty quickly, communication gaps would start to emerge that would contribute to our product not meeting expectations from our users, internal and/or external stakeholders. In the long run, there would be a lack of clear direction and cohesion for where the product is heading, which would result in resources being put toward wrong or unnecessary initiatives in the business.

Product Manager, Product Owner, Project Lead. All these similar titles and roles. What's the actual difference and do we really need all of them at every company or product?

**Nina:**
I haven't worked at a company with Product Owners and Project Leads, so I don't have experience with that. But I think it depends a lot on company size, how it's organized, what methods you use, and what type of product you're selling.

**Viktor:**
Well, you know what they say—a beloved child has many names. What you call it depends a lot on the type of company, its size, and how you choose to work within it. Let's start with the difference between Product Manager and Project Manager. A project manager handles a specific project—leading the work within the scope of that project—whereas a Product Manager leads the development of a product. As a rule, a product owner has a broader, more long-term perspective in also maintaining, changing, and refining. A product is an organism whereas a project is a boxed solution. The difference lies in managing those different relationships. Projects are requirement-driven while products are need-driven.

As a PM, you're involved in many parts of the company and product. How do you get everyone working toward the same goal and avoid ending up in silos?

**Nina:**
It's difficult, and it's very easy for each team to only think about their part of the product. I believe it's important to have a shared goal and a clear product vision. I try to remind everyone of the goals we have, communicate to the teams about what's happening in different parts of the company, and encourage teams to talk a lot with each other and our customers.

**Viktor:**
Involve everyone in the group already during the exploratory phase so there isn't too much "hand-off." I've seen many organizations that think they're working iteratively but only work iteratively within their own team or work group. Then there's still a hand-off to the next team and so on. The end result becomes something completely different from the original purpose. I use the North Star framework to connect the work we do to something measurable and see how we successively improve the core values of the product. So involve as many people as possible from the start, base it on user needs, set measurable goals for what we want to improve, and it's easier to keep the group together and create creative work.

Business, technology, and design are considered the three cornerstones of product development. How do you ensure that design is valued equally with the other parts and not just seen as a pretty package?

**Nina:**
I think part of it is about communication—getting the organization to understand that design isn't just packaging but the entire user experience. At Funnel, Design is a very new function, but just as we have a Chief Product Officer and a Chief Technology Officer, we now have a Chief Design Officer, and all three sit on the leadership team. Having Design with an equally strong voice as Product and Development, I think makes it easier to ensure it's valued equally.

**Viktor:**
By highlighting and clarifying throughout the entire organization the value of a good experience for your users and customers. You can have the world's coolest tech, but if nobody understands how to use it, you'll miss the target. Same thing on the business side. If you haven't understood the end user's needs, you'll have a short-lived product with high maintenance costs. Far too many products fail because they try to give users a solution instead of solving their fundamental needs. That's where Design comes in. It often starts with your marketing. Explaining what you solve in a good way and setting the right expectations. I work incredibly closely with marketing to create the best possible conditions.

Customers always have tons of often conflicting requests. How do you choose who to listen to and what to prioritize?

**Nina:**
I usually start by trying to understand what underlying problem they want to solve with their request, and then find out if other customers have the same problem. I also look at what type of customer it is and how well their request fits our strategy and top priorities. I also talk with the developers to understand what potential solutions exist and how much work it would be to build them.

**Viktor:**
I think you should listen to all your customers' requests, but not equate them with requirements. Instead, figure out what the real need is. Customers have lots of "wants," but you should work with their "needs" through activities like exploration, user research, prototyping, user testing, and verification. Verifying before you start "building" your product always pays off in the end.

What do you wish you'd known at the beginning of your PM career?

**Nina:**
I think I'm still at the beginning of my PM career :)

**Viktor:**
That as a product manager, it's not about you personally coming up with solutions to your customers' needs or thinking up clever features. Your role is to focus on leading and facilitating the process that leads to a better product. I would have liked someone to clearly and simply explain all the important product metrics you need to have down cold. Sometimes I see myself very much as a conductor directing an orchestra where I'm not playing the instruments myself. I make sure it's a really good and enjoyable experience both during rehearsal and for the audience.

Finally, do you have any good tips on books, podcasts, or TED talks for someone who wants to learn more about the role?

**Nina:**
Marty Cagan has written the book *Inspired*, which describes the PM role well. His blog "Insights" on www.svpg.com is also very good.
I've started work training. One day a week I'm supposed to leave the safety of home and sit at Park Söder with a few of my colleagues. IRL. Completely insane. ✌️😻😅🌞🍁

title: Amazons bästa produkt är ett skämt
text:
# Amazon's Best Product Is a Joke

Amazon's Best Product Is a Joke
Created on 2021-10-27 16:35
Published on 2021-10-27 17:27

A warning. I'm about to dissect a really good SNL sketch, and in doing so it will probably stop being funny. If you haven't already seen it, do that before you continue reading.

Amazon Silver on SNL.

I had a good laugh when I saw the sketch the first time. Pensioners are so (unintentionally) funny. The new technology, so obvious to us, is like magic to an old person. They don't understand anything anyway.

Then it hit me. The reason it's funny is that the combination of old people and new technology creates such a contrast. But why do they have to be opposites? Advanced, voice-controlled AI devices weren't developed for grins and giggles. Rarely does a unicorn startup brag about seniors as their primary target demographic. Strange, since more and more people are becoming older and older, so there should be money to be made. The paradox is that many of the elderly's needs are actually satisfied by Alexa Silver. So instead of laughing at the old folks, we should kick-start production. We have ed-tech and med-tech now—what we need is old-tech.

So what would Amazon Silver offer?

**Generous margins for error.**

You can say Alexa, Odessa, Alexandra, or Collessa. The device understands. Maybe you could set your own name. It understands what you mean even if the instructions come in the wrong order or are incoherent.

**Clarity.**

It speaks loudly and clearly, doesn't mind repeating itself and rephrasing. It understands that different contexts have different meanings.

**Companionship.**

Loneliness is a major problem that hits the elderly especially hard. Family rarely visits, friends are few and far between, and any care staff are always rushing off to the next client. Here, a constantly present conversation partner—or at least a listening ear—would be perfect.

**Health and wellbeing.**

How is it that people die alone and it takes days before anyone discovers it? A smart device could keep tabs on how the person is doing, remind them about medication, monitor their movement around the apartment, regulate the temperature, measure heart rate (combined with a smartwatch), call for help, and much more. The device could encourage activity if someone has been sitting too long or suggest contact if they haven't spoken to anyone in a while.

**Personal assistant.**

Finally someone to help remind about appointments like doctor's visits, medication, and when relatives are coming to visit. Make it easy to locate important things like phones, wallets, and keys. Help with shopping, booking a hairdresser, transportation services, family visits, etc.

All this functionality could probably be achieved today if you're technically gifted and have the time and money required. But I haven't seen it all gathered and packaged in a sleek, user-friendly product at an affordable price, where everything just works out of the box. Until then, the idea remains just a sketch on SNL and we'll keep laughing at these silly old folks. Like here: OK Goo Goo. ;-)

The simple question "Which design is best" can actually be really tricky to answer. Yet that's precisely what many designers want to know. Here I reflect on everything that's wrong with the question and what you can do instead. #design #designers #feedback

title: Vilken design är bäst?
text:
# Which Design Is Best?

Which is better? That's a question that often pops up on various design forums online. A couple of fairly similar design proposals sit side by side and people are asked to vote for their favorite. Nothing strange about that, you might think. It's surely an excellent way to gather feedback.

We can start by looking at how you can even answer such a question. How do I know what is good or bad? There are two ways to think about it.

## The Objective Rulebook

Here you start from a universal standard to assess the qualities of the design. The better the design has followed these rules, the more points it gets on the scale. There are plenty of rules for typography, layout, color theory, graphic form, photography, communication, and usability. But which of these scales should you follow? A high score in one can be really low in another. Rules are meant to be challenged and broken. How should I know if the designer broke them out of ignorance or deliberately to create a certain effect?

## The Subjective Gut Feeling

Design's superpower is that it speaks to our emotions; whether we like something or not, a reaction occurs. Design reaches our subconscious, our experiences and preferences. But if gut feeling is subjective, then it's always right, isn't it? No one can question that my feelings aren't valid. If I don't like pink, there's a good chance I won't like your pink design. Does that make the design bad then? But if someone else loves pink? Does the design suddenly become good again? Then the question isn't about which design is better, but which preferences are most common.

The design's purpose could also be to create negative feelings or to provoke—for example, a picture of a starving polar bear. Can the design be good even if I don't like it or how it makes me feel? Will I be able to distinguish between my feelings and the design's quality?

What is considered stylish and beautiful also varies over time. Today's hottest trend is tomorrow's bottom-tier fashion. Does the design's value and quality diminish over time then? Or is the design still good even if it's not as sought after?

## Tell Me What You Want, What You Really, Really Want

Another problem is the result of the question itself. There's no consensus on how respondents answer the question. Are they marketers, typographers, or UX designers? Are they basing their judgment on gut feeling or ten years of experience? Are they cautious or brutally honest? When you mix all the answers and perspectives together, the result becomes both difficult to interpret and misleading.

When and whom you ask also matters. One day 56 prefer version A and 67 prefer B. The next day 82 liked A but only 49 liked B. What does that mean? Who should you trust?

Just because one option is better than the other doesn't mean either of them is good. Both might be completely worthless and will miss the target regardless of the voting results.

## Context Is King

Assessing design without any context, background, or purpose is both difficult and pointless. Just because one design appeals to me more doesn't mean it will resonate with the intended target audience. Is there even an intended target audience? Put the Swedish Tax Agency's website next to Nike.com. Everyone can say which one is cooler or more entertaining. But does that automatically mean Nike.com is better? Why not take the cool design and paint over the Tax Agency's site with celebrities and beautiful models? Or is it the case that both are good at what they're supposed to do and design is just a way for them to do their job?

In other words, design is subordinate to purpose, and without knowing the purpose, you can't assess how good the design is. The Tax Agency's site can be stiff and boring but still be excellently designed. Stylish doesn't automatically mean good. Say what?

Design without a purpose is just an empty shell, a gift wrapper. It becomes a template waiting to be put in the right context. But if you as a designer start with a clear purpose, target audience, problem you're solving, and needs you're meeting, there's an excellent way to know which design is better. Test it on real users and you won't have to ask the audience.

I've been there. Had my sketches torn to shreds by malicious critics and design illiterates. Or was I just overreacting? Were they right? Does it mean I'm completely worthless? Nooooooooo...."
"#design #feedback #critique #designer

title: Designkritik är inte en förolämpning
text:
# Design Criticism Is Not an Insult

Design Criticism Is Not an Insult
Created on 2022-02-22 11:37
Published on 2022-02-22 12:17

We creatives have been taught the importance of marketing and selling ourselves. Be your own brand, so to speak. The customer buys you, not what you sell. Follow your heart and your passion. Find your own style, your voice and identity. Be proud of your work and know your worth. Good and well-intentioned advice, but with the side effect that you as a person, your skills and what you create all blend together. It becomes completely impossible to discuss the design without simultaneously evaluating you as a person.

So what can we do to sever the emotional bond to our work? How can we strengthen our confidence so we aren't crushed by all the "well-meaning" criticism? How can we see a design presentation as something positive and not an interrogation?

Here are some insights that have helped me get better at receiving criticism.

It's not about you

You've struggled hard with your sketches, poured blood, sweat and tears into them. Of course you want your work to be well received. But here comes the twist. Your design is created by you but not for you. It will belong to the client, the developers and the users. Don't start the presentation with "now I'm going to present *my* design" but rather "here is a design solution to the problem."

It's teamwork

Even if you're the only designer in the project, the team, or the entire company, it's not you alone who should solve the whole problem. The others, regardless of role or title, are there with you. Include them early and often so everyone feels involved. Suddenly everyone is in on it and shares responsibility for a successful outcome. It's not you and your design against the world, but you and your team against the problem, where design is an important part of the solution.

Design is important but not everything

Design may be your whole world. You've studied it for years—everything from typography and color theory to usability. It's big, it's difficult, and there are many decisions to make. But design is "only" one part of the final result. There are other parts that come before and after that are just as big and difficult as yours. Make sure you understand the entire process so your design stays relevant. Don't get hurt if people don't appreciate the greatness of your font choices or elegant layout. Instead, focus on what value it creates and how it helps you reach your goal.

Be prepared

Sometimes feedback can come spontaneously when you're not ready. Then it's important to simply thank them for the input without becoming defensive. The most common scenario is planned meetings with the client, the team, or other stakeholders. Think through what questions might come up and how you can answer them as objectively as possible. What's the best outcome from the meeting and what can you do to make it happen? What's the worst-case scenario, how likely is it, and what can you do to prevent it? Mentally prepare yourself to stay calm and relaxed throughout the entire meeting.

Be empathetic

When empathy and design are mentioned in the same breath, it's almost exclusively about the users. Rarely does anyone mention empathy for your client, your team members, or yourself. But your design has consequences before it even reaches the users. Think about the developer who gets your sketches dumped in their lap, often without having had any say in it, and now must somehow magically transform your visions into reality. Show that you care about their situation and how you can make their day easier through your design choices. Does that animated button really contribute to a better experience or does it just add complexity and development time?

And don't forget yourself. With better self-awareness and a little distance from yourself, you'll find it easier to handle setbacks and let your overblown ego take a back seat.

title: Systemet som Gud (Daniel Kahneman) glömde
text:
# The System That God (Daniel Kahneman) Forgot

Created on 2022-03-30 14:37
Published on 2022-03-30 14:55

I really appreciate Susan Weinschenk's new course in behavioral design on Ambition Empower. The first lesson was an introduction to how we make our decisions. If you're familiar with Daniel Kahneman's book, Thinking, Fast and Slow, then you know the concept of the two ways of thinking. System 1, the fast, energy-efficient way that relies on habits, impulses and unconscious decisions. System 2, the slow and analytical way that requires time and energy and is reserved for difficult or important decisions. We use one or the other. Either we go on autopilot or we stop and think it through. Both ways are very good and complement each other excellently. The only negative thing about the concept is the confusing nomenclature. Wouldn't fast and slow have worked, like in the book title? Now I have to think slowly every time I need to remember which system is which.

What do I mean by system 3? If system 1 is our habits and system 2 is our logical thinking, then system 3 is the human factor where our personality, emotions and ego have the final say. System 3 is where system 1 and system 2 meet, both mathematically and conceptually. System 3 kicks in where logic no longer suffices, like which partner you should choose or deciding which kitten is the cutest. These are deliberate choices based on gut feeling rather than logic.

According to Susan, we use either one or the other system when we make a choice. Therefore, a product should be designed based on which system will be used most. For example, a bag of chips should entice impulse purchases and is hardly an important decision. Typical system 1. The design needs to be expressive, easy to understand and speak directly to the customer. If, on the other hand, we're designing for choosing a pension fund, the priorities would be completely different. Here we need to create security and trust and provide a lot of information before the user can make a choice.

For system 3, we take all the feelings from system 1 and let them override all the logic in system 2. A perfect example is the original iPhone. When it was presented, it was inferior to its competitors on almost every point. It lacked a physical keyboard, had poor reception, terrible battery life and only a handful of pre-installed apps. Yet it got customers to look past all its shortcomings and yearn to swipe their fingers across that (at the time) enormous screen.

All luxury goods require a major (financial) decision but the seller doesn't want you to start thinking too much at the same time. That's why they sneak past your common sense (for example by not even showing or mentioning the price) and go straight for your ego. Why should you buy this extremely expensive watch or this ridiculously gas-guzzling sports car? Because you're worth it. Because you can finally be yourself. Because everyone else will see you. Because no Nobel Prize winner will come and tell you how you should and shouldn't think. Fast or slow? I think the way I want to. So there.

title: 7 habits of highly mediocre people
text:
# 7 Habits of Highly Mediocre People

Created on 2022-05-28 10:30
Published on 2022-05-28 11:00

## 1. Define the scale yourself

If you're classified as mediocre, it's because you're being measured against a scale that someone else has set. You can either accept this and follow the rules of the game. If it's important to you to be the best at something, create your own scale. You have every opportunity to become your company's master of Fortnite or beer tasting.

## 2. What matters most isn't measurable

The truly important values in life cannot be measured. You can't be the best at love or win the world championship in happiness. In other words, we're all superstars or mediocrities. These values are also highly individual. What makes one person happy doesn't necessarily work for anyone else. Only you know what works for you. You can always decide to become the best parent, friend, or boss that you personally can be.

## 3. Efficiency isn't efficient

We're constantly urged to become more efficient, agile, productive, and innovative. With new technology, we create new opportunities to churn out even more products. We seek the promised land of happiness that eternal growth has promised us. But the sad truth is that we don't become happier, just more stressed, and we squander our own resources and the planet's completely unnecessarily. What if the answer is the opposite? Instead of working more, work less and spend that time on something that actually makes you happy. (With respect for all single parents who struggle to pay the rent.)

## The world needs mediocre people

Zlatan needs his fans far more than the fans need Zlatan. It's us who create and carry the superstars. What would all the TikTok stars be without their fans and likes? Who would consume all that content? You and I play an important role in the equation. It's thanks to us ordinary people that superstars exist. Next time it should be you signing Zlatan's jersey.

## It's uneconomical to be the best

It costs an extreme amount to become the best. Most people aren't willing to sacrifice what it takes to reach the top. Many try, but few succeed. If you somehow make it, you have nothing left to win or prove, but everything to lose. It takes just as much energy to stay at the top and fight off everyone else who wants your place.

Instead of mastering just one thing, you can use that same time and energy to become completely decent or even really good at many different areas. You also avoid betting on the wrong horse. Being an expert on combustion engines today might not be the most fun thing.

## You are your own superstar

A superstar as inspiration can be motivating, but the realization that you'll never reach their level is demoralizing. If you constantly compare yourself to the best, you'll always feel inadequate. Why even try? No wonder most kids quit their sports or hobbies when all the fun disappears and all that's left are demands and high expectations.

If you instead start from yourself, you are right now always your best self. You don't need to do anything to be the best, win prizes or titles. You do it because you want to.

## Embrace your inner mediocrity

Words like mediocre, middling, half-hearted, and acceptable have a negative ring to them. They suggest that something or someone hasn't lived up to their full potential. At the same time, there are words like balanced, measured, moderate, and sufficient. These suggest that you've done enough but haven't overdone it. The same thing but with two completely different values. Now it's up to you what you choose to fill your inner mediocrity with. Fill it up, but not to the brim. There should always be room for a little more.

## A few tips:

Want inspiration? Read *The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People*.

Want advice that works and makes you appreciate what you have? Read *The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck*.

title: Vad kunden egentligen vill ha och hur du ska ta reda på det.
text:
# What the Customer Really Wants and How to Find Out

Have you also heard that it's important to deliver beyond the customer's expectations? Usually that's interpreted to mean you should work extra hard, give 110%. Nothing wrong with that. But if everyone else is also giving 110%, then that's what customers expect. So your 110% is just 100% in the customer's eyes. Then you'd need to give 120% for it to be noticed. Meanwhile, you only get paid for 100%. In other words, you're giving away a fifth of your work for free. That sounds like a bad deal and a shortcut to burnout.

What's even stranger is that most people who want to deliver beyond customer expectations often don't even know what the customer actually expects. It's like doing the high jump with an invisible bar. How the hell are you supposed to know how high to aim? There's nothing more frustrating than when you've put in a lot of work, followed the customer's wishes to the letter, and get the response: "No, that's not what I wanted."

The tricky thing about expectations is that they're usually unspoken. Especially when they form the basis for an assignment or project. It's easy to fall into the trap of thinking that detailed requirement specs, success metrics, lists of features, and budgets also fulfill the customer's expectations. But they're just hygiene factors. Expectations lie beyond these documents, sometimes embedded in the customer's subconscious. That's why every major project should start with this simple question:

**What does the customer expect will happen when the project is complete?**

Forget for a moment all the obstacles, all the features that need to be built, all the timelines that need to be met, or problems that need to be solved. Stop selling, pitching, planning, and presenting for a moment. Transport yourself and the customer into the future. The project is done, everything has gone according to plan, and the expected effects have taken hold. What is different now than before the project?

Your only job is to understand why this vision matters to the customer. What does it mean for the users, the business, and the brand? What new opportunities open up? What would happen if the project didn't happen or failed? Write down all the effects and outcomes the customer is hoping for. No matter how big or small they seem.

The next question is just as important, and this is where we bring the customer back down to earth. Now it's time to start kicking the tires and turning those fluffy dreams into something concrete.

**What needs to happen to make it real?**

The idea isn't that you'll end up with a detailed timeline. The key points are enough. Together you can distinguish between what's a requirement and what's a vague hope. Are the customer's expectations realistic, or do they need to be adjusted? Are there things outside your control? Which are really difficult or time-consuming? Which are the most risky? What do we need to tackle first? Can we break the journey into different stages?

The final task is to distill the expectation down to one or a couple of sentences. What's absolutely the most important thing that needs to happen for the customer to feel satisfied? This becomes the customer's MVE—Minimal Viable Expectations. Now it's just a matter of regularly verifying throughout the project that your delivery actually meets the customer's MVE. If there's too big a gap, the work or MVE needs to be adjusted ASAP. The important thing is that the delivery and expectations are in sync when the project reaches the finish line.

Still want to deliver beyond the customer's expectations? Just include one of the less important points and add it as a bonus. Mission accomplished.

title: Tre sätt att fly funktionsfabriken
text:
# Three Ways to Escape the Feature Factory

Created on 2022-09-09 12:50
Published on 2022-09-09 13:36

A digital product must constantly evolve, or it dies—especially now that most are transformed into services requiring a monthly subscription. User expectations are to have access to all existing and upcoming features as long as they pay. More features mean more value for money.

## The majority of your work is wasted

When you ask users what they want, the answer is usually new features. Products with more features also seem more powerful than those with fewer. They sell better. More features sound like an obvious win-win and is the most common way to develop a product. In reality, only a fraction of all new features are actually used. Up to 80% are rarely or never used (according to Pendo 2019). So much work for so little impact.

## More features lead to greater complexity

The problem isn't just that new features go unused. They make the product more complex, harder to navigate, and slower, while users simultaneously expect unchanged simplicity and clarity. For example, Instagram started as a sleek social app. With few features and a focus on images, it was easy to understand and simple to use. Take a photo, apply a filter, share, like, comment. With additional features like stories, reels, and shop, the app and number of functions have grown. Logically, you could argue it's become better since you can now do so much more. At the same time, complexity has increased and the mental model has become far more convoluted. The simple question "What should I use this app for" suddenly became very difficult to answer. If the app were a physical product instead, it would resemble the creation below.

So what's the solution?

Should we stop listening and acting on customers, users, and the market? Of course not. No one would buy a new iPhone if it was exactly the same as the previous model. We can't just halt development. Here are three paths to developing your product in a more constructive way.

## 1. Needs before features

Every new product starts by solving a problem better, more efficiently, or more simply than existing solutions. This is the very reason for the product's existence, its USP or "Jobs to be done." Unfortunately, many products lose focus along the way and the original purpose gets buried under a constantly growing pile of new features. The simple becomes complex, the quick becomes cumbersome.

Instead of asking what features are demanded, focus on how we can help users achieve their goals better or faster. We also need to keep track of users' actual needs—whether they're changing or if new ones are emerging.

Questions to ask:
- What are the most important needs the product satisfies?
- How can we make the product do that as well as possible?
- How do we ensure the product remains relevant in the future?

## 2. Structure

There are ways to make a large and complex product feel accessible. The trick is not to overwhelm the user's cognitive capacity. Logical structure, text and naming that are easy to understand and remember, consistent UX, and above all, hiding features that aren't needed at the moment. Photoshop lets me switch workspaces with tools tailored to whichever task I'm currently working on. Photo editing has one set of tools, illustration another. I can also create custom workspaces that suit my specific needs. Everything else is hidden but available if I need it.

Structure can also mean splitting up a product that has become too large, like when Messenger and Facebook became two separate apps. Another example is Photoshop, which couldn't meet the new needs of UX designers. Adobe chose to create XD from scratch—a much simpler and faster tool entirely focused on creating prototypes and flows.

In some cases, the way forward is the opposite: combining or integrating products. Think of all the data entry workers who have to report in one system and then cut and paste everything into another. Here you want a system that handles it all, or systems that communicate with each other. Users shouldn't have to pay the price for poor IT solutions.

Questions to ask:
- Are there major differences in how different roles, purposes, or tasks use your product?
- Can we split up or structure the product in a way that makes it easier for users to understand and use?
- Can we streamline users' daily work by reducing duplicate work, multiple logins, and the number of systems they use?

## 3. Work with what you have

Your product is only as good as the amount of value it creates for the user. In other words, the better and more effectively your product is used, the more value it creates. If users learn to use your product's existing features in a better way, the product automatically becomes more valuable. The best part is you don't have to create a single new feature. Magic. Note that this isn't about increasing the amount of time users spend in the app. It's about the user's benefit, not your shareholders'.

Another way is to update the experience. The same old product can feel brand new with a little visual refresh. Small improvements can be enough—like creating consistency in colors, language, and UX patterns. Clear out all the hundreds of shades of gray that have crept into the product and make sure the language is consistent and on brand. Build a design system if you don't already have one.

The language app Duolingo redesigned its appearance without changing its content or functionality. The result is still a completely new experience.

Questions to ask:
- How can we help users use our product even better?
- Can we make our product more attractive, clear, or accessible through copy and UI?
- How can we ensure the experience remains consistent over time and permeates the entire product?

## Finally, choose your features carefully

New features aren't evil; they're what drive development forward. Just don't let them be the only way to solve every problem. Users don't want more buttons (regardless of what they say). They want better ways to solve their problems. Make sure that's what you're building instead of yet another feature that no one will use.

Introduction to Mental Models

Pendo report
https://www.pendo.io/resources/the-2019-feature-adoption-report/

I have had many duels with both my inner "Imposter syndrome" and Janteloven. Sometimes one at a time, sometimes both simultaneously. We have argued, wrestled and fought. "I am the Law" shouts Jante as he charges at me. An inner struggle that is very difficult and often destructive. Above all, the outcome is very uncertain. There are many doubts and I am only one.

That's why I've changed my strategy. When Imposter syndrome or Jante start causing trouble, I know that I have left my comfort zone and their job is to bring me back. They want to keep me safe, not see me fail or embarrass myself. Maybe they're not my enemies but overprotective friends who mean well, just in the wrong way.

So I sit down with Jante and the Imposter, bring out coffee and pastries. Then I ask them what they're so worried about. What's the worst that can happen? How can we make sure it doesn't happen or handle the consequences if it does? Then I just let them talk out their worries.

No more kicks and blows. We'll work it out together.

#impostersyndrome #jante #fika #selfconfidence

Imagine seeing the words "Move fast and break things" painted on the wall of a doctor's office, pharmaceutical company, or tax agency. Crazy, right?

"If I'm going to have surgery, I need to know with confidence that the surgeon knows what they're doing. The medicine for chickenpox for my child shouldn't be an MVP."

We have very high standards when it comes to certain areas. Medicine is tested thoroughly, doctors have had ethical guidelines for over a hundred years. When it comes to IT, most things are still the Wild West. Companies add ethics, security, and trust as an update only when things start going wrong. But it's much harder once the genie is out of the bottle. New medicine assumes that unexpected side effects will emerge, both in the short and long term. You know it's impossible to account for every possibility. Which app developer does that? By then they're already on to the next project or feature without time to look back.

The future will never move as slowly as it does right now. So we don't need to push any harder, rather we need to create some friction and reflection. Sacrifice some efficiency in favor of empathy, security, and ethics.

The only ones who can still run fast and break things are our children. Maybe the occasional pet. 😻

#meta #empathy #movefast

Short version: Ego is a poor substitute for self-confidence.

Long version:

Ego is like empty calories, it tastes good, gives a sense of fullness and you just want more. But ego only takes up space without providing any nourishment in return. Few people around you become inspired by your self-absorption.

An ego is not interested in giving, encouraging, lifting others up or collaborating, unless it directly benefits yourself. An ego's nourishment is attention and praise. The more the better. Sharing means a loss and stings.

Ego is a poor substitute for self-confidence. Especially early in your career, insecurity can be hidden behind a pasted-on ego. It's a destructive way to protect a person's true self because the ego eventually takes over.

At the same time, the ego is an unreliable ally. The bigger it gets, the more vulnerable it becomes. Eventually there are threats and trigger points everywhere. It requires more and more energy to defend the imagined territorial boundaries when everyone around you is perceived as a threat.

Instead, you can:
- Share. Knowledge, praise, ideas, time in the spotlight.
- Reflect and ask for feedback. The best way to gain self-insight and work away your trigger points.
- Humor and self-distance. Soften the sharp edges of your ego and transform your demanding ego into a strong personality.
- Become a beginner. Learn something new far from your area of expertise. Being truly terrible at something gives perspective.
- Apologize. If you (or your ego) have caused harm, tears or hurt feelings, there's no better way to both start building trust and tear down the ego.

Yes you can. 😍

#energy #ego #humble #impostersyndrome #sharing #confidence

It was nerve-wracking but exciting to talk live and IRL in front of a bunch of attentive UX students. Nice that I got to share the stage with smart people like Linda Stenmark and Ulrika Lundin, they made sure to add quality not just quantity. ✌

We talked about ethics from our professional perspective, how we think about the subject, what methods we use but above all how we make sure something actually happens in practice.

My insights from the panel discussion:

- There's more talk about ethics now than ever before. However, there's still a need for more constructive discussions at all levels within organizations but also concrete ways to go from words to action.

- Ethical thinking should be included from the start when you build a product, not slapped on afterwards. What consequences will the product create if/when it's released?

- Ethics should be an integrated part of the business idea, not empty promises in the sales pitch. You can make money AND be ethical at the same time. It's possible to work ethically AND be profitable.

Big thanks to Sharon Lindberg and Teresa Cerratto Pargman at Stockholm University for letting me come. And good luck to all students, our future rests in your hands. No pressure. 😅

You've promised yourself that you'd start jogging again. Now it's morning and a pretty good opportunity to get down to it. You ask yourself: "Should I go for a run today?"

You want to go out but you're feeling a bit tired, you worked late, maybe you're coming down with something. Better to skip it today and run extra far next time. Suddenly you're bargaining with yourself, you find pros and cons, start weighing them against each other. Even if you land on a "Yes," you need to make sure it actually happens. Otherwise, you're just widening the gap between what you want to do and what you actually do.

Ask this instead: "How do I make sure the jog happens today?"

No energy is wasted on questioning or searching for arguments. "No" isn't even on the agenda. The focus is on making sure it gets done. How long or how far doesn't matter. The important thing is that it happens.

When you apply for a job, you're actually applying for several jobs at the same time. There's the job you get when you start, but you're also looking for your next job, the one you're heading toward. If there's no next step, well then you won't stay at the company when it's time to advance.

Then you're back to job hunting and the company has to start looking for a replacement. Nobody wins.

It's of great importance for both of you that the employment is as long and rewarding as possible. You signal that you're coming to stay and won't take off as soon as something better comes along. Explore what opportunities exist for training, education and the next step in your career.

- How much time and money will you get to spend on further education?

- What is the likely development for you in both the short and long term?

- How can you both increase your skills and generate more value for the company?

- Are there any good examples of employees who started where you are now and developed in different directions?

See employment as a relationship. Do you see a future with the other party? Can you develop together and have a happy future together? The answer to these questions is more important than whether you can maximize your salary or whether there's a ping pong table.

TLDR: I've taken the well-known monopoly experiment and given it a twist."

"Imagine that life is a game, like monopoly. All players are divided into two teams, team A (oranges) and team B (bananas). The same rules and game board apply to both teams. The only difference is that team A has been given an extra die while team B has to play with just one. The teams don't know about this, and they can't see the dice rolls themselves. What they see is the result on the board, how far everyone's pieces move and what decisions the players make."

"Without much surprise, things consistently go better for team A, although there are exceptions. Some oranges roll really poorly and make questionable decisions while some bananas get lucky and make the best use of their resources. But no matter how well the bananas play, they don't reach the top tier. The results from just one die simply aren't enough."

"The bananas are unhappy and claim that something is wrong with the game. Even though the dice rolls are hidden, it's not hard to see the clear differences. Some bananas even accuse the oranges of cheating and want them to give back the properties and hotels."

"The oranges respond that they certainly follow the rules and that the results are a combination of luck and skill. They point to their teammates who haven't done so well and then to the occasional successful banana. 'See there, we don't always win. And you CAN definitely win, you just need to stop complaining, fight harder, get better, smarter, braver and more skilled.'"

"Both teams have followed the rules and done their best. Some had luck, others bad luck, some made good decisions and others didn't. Yet the results came out differently, where the preconditions played a much larger role than individual performance."

"The only way to solve the problem is to adjust the preconditions. Don't punish the oranges, don't take away their hotels or extra die. Instead, give the bananas another die. The situation will still be objectively unfair but the differences will steadily decrease."

"I know I'm oversimplifying a complex problem. At the same time, I'm convinced that many (oranges) are still unaware of these 'invisible' differences. Those who haven't looked up, chosen to turn a blind eye or didn't dare speak up. They need to be part of this to achieve lasting structural change, not just quick fixes or awareness campaigns."

"The customer is always right" is an old "truth" from the service industry. Somewhat ironic since everyone (including customers) knows it's a white lie. The truth is rather the opposite. Customers are often wrong, don't know what they want, what's best for them, and lack design expertise.

Letting the customer tell you what to do is usually a sure path to disaster. If customers already knew what they wanted and could do it themselves, they wouldn't come to you. You're the expert and need to take that role. Your job is NOT to do what the customer says but to interpret what the customer says and then show how you can solve the problem through design.

However, the customer ALWAYS has the right to say no. It doesn't matter how good your ideas are or how nice your sketches are if the customer isn't on board. After all, they're the ones paying and they're the ones who have to face the consequences if things don't go as planned.

Getting a NO can even push you to find other and better solutions.

- What a strange question. That's not how we measure UX."
"- OK. Can I get two meters of UX?"
"- Now you need to get serious. We measure UX per hour."
"- Oh, like speed? How far can you get in 5 hours of UX?"
"- Well, that depends on how experienced, skilled and efficient the person is. How well it works in the team, how much bureaucracy you have to deal with. Plus a bit of luck, whether you find the right solution right away."
"- So one hour of UX can basically be anything."
"- Yes, but you measure results."
"- Hmm… OK. Can I get three kWh of UX then."
""
"Why have we gotten stuck on hourly rates when everyone talks about value, results and experience? Time is always important, but the measurement should be the other way around. The faster the better, the more results per hour, the higher the hourly cost. Like sending a letter by regular mail or express. Same result, but the faster way costs more. Nothing strange about that."
""
"The UX/hour model doesn't encourage innovation or efficiency. The perfect consultant does what's expected (maybe slightly more) in precisely the allocated time. But you can never know in advance how fast or slow the design work will go. There are far too many 'it depends' factors."
""
"Project orders have become like buying candy in bulk. We take a bit away from the licorice (research) and grab some raspberry boats (dev) instead. So, now we're satisfied, we go and weigh it."
"But you'd never open an Aladdin box in a shop, remove the pieces you don't like and then demand a lower price at the checkout."
"Nor would you go to the cinema for half price and only watch half the film. The purpose disappears, despite the discount."
""
"This isn't the clients' or consultants' fault, but how the game is rigged, what gets measured and what gets rewarded."
""
"Value based pricing is still like a mirage in the distance. But some have succeeded, and even done really well, far exceeding the locked hourly rate model. VBP doesn't suit every company or project. At the same time, it should be just as obvious an alternative as selling UX per kilo, I mean per hour."
""
"For more inspiration, check out Jonathan Courtney at AJ&Smart, Chris Do (Futur) and Blair Enns (Win Without Pitching)."
""
"#ux #innovation #inspiration #valuebasedpricing

You read that correctly. There is nothing wrong with the men themselves. That would have been a good explanation or excuse. Maybe something that could be fixed through exercise or a better diet.

There is nothing wrong with their bodies or their minds either. They are fully functional people who act of their own free will. The problem is not that they are white or that they are men. Not even that they are offended—everyone can feel offended at some point. Attacking the fact that white privileged men are white privileged men is pointless. It is not something anyone can change, even if one wanted to.

It is the behavior and attitude that is the problem.

Behaviors do not arise from a vacuum, they are nothing we are born with or afflicted by. They are something we learn. Behaviors that are rewarded and encouraged become stronger. Behaviors that yield no benefit or are punished fade away.

The question "What is wrong with white, offended men" is therefore misdirected.

What we should instead ask ourselves is:
- How is it that this behavior (still) exists?
- How do we ensure that the behavior is not rewarded or accepted?
- What behavior do we want to encourage instead?

#clickbait #behavior #privilege #men

"Trust" has become a trendy value word for many organizations. In itself, that's very good, and together with psychological safety, it's the foundation for a healthy workplace.

But what does the word mean for you as an organization? Does it mean the same thing for everyone, does it include your customers, and do the same rules apply across different parts of the organization regardless of role and title?

At one of my previous workplaces, it meant that you shouldn't question your managers or their decisions. You were just supposed to trust that they knew best, after all they were managers for a reason. Unfortunately, I lacked confidence in this definition and left. 😅

More important questions are:
- What trust do we have today?
- How do we measure that what we believe is true?
- How do you concretely work to build, maintain and improve trust?
- Who is responsible, sees to it that...?
- What do we do when someone experiences problems regarding trust?

When trust flows in both directions in the organizational hierarchy, it should lead to more autonomy for the teams. Let them solve problems instead of just delivering on orders.

1. Trust the process
2. People over process
3. Trust the people

#people #process #trust #definition

Every royal court of rank had its own court jester. Beyond being the Netflix of its time and providing entertainment, the jester had another important role. They could tell uncomfortable truths disguised as jokes and songs. Say things that no one else dared to say, even joke about the king himself without losing their head. The reason was that the jester posed no threat. They had no status and could not compete for power. They were part of the court but simultaneously stood outside it.

Today, many organizations lack a court jester, especially large organizations with great distance between top and bottom. Because bad news rarely travels upward in the hierarchy, those at the top usually know the least. Poor information, no overview, no one speaking up. No wonder scandals follow one after another.

It is very few people in a board who would stand up and say: "Excuse me gentlemen, but this initiative is pure greenwashing. There is a 72% risk it will be exposed within five years. The estimated gain is 2 million against an estimated loss of 100 billion in shares, damages and damaged brand. There is a 56% risk that up to 5000 people will be negatively affected. I advise you not to proceed."

Imagine if these executives had someone to consult before making such a decision. Someone who dared to tell it like it is, without fear of losing their head, I mean their job. Someone who could cut through all silos, egos, titles, hierarchies and politics. Gather information from different parts of the company and present it objectively, clearly and truthfully. Doesn't even have to be funny or in rhyme.

If we build AI the right way, with the right goals and data, its lack of human characteristics can actually be a great asset. It can complement us where we fall short, put us back on track when we waver. When the inner moral compass fails, an AI-based jester can come to the rescue. Then maybe we become better at listening and ensure we avoid one disaster or another.

I'm mingling around and spot an old colleague. We haven't seen each other in nearly a hundred years. I walk over and say hello: "Nice to see you, it's been a while. So where are you working now?"

A moment of awkward silence. The cheerful mood retreats. The standard question has become a minefield. I get it. We start talking about something else.

There's something strange about how we view our jobs and titles. It's such a big part of your identity that you even put it in small letters before your name. Working is doing right by yourself. Not working is consciously or unconsciously associated with laziness, incompetence and exclusion. What's odd is that I felt that way about myself, even when I knew my dismissal was due to something else. I couldn't even go out for drinks with my (newly old) colleagues. I felt like a failure because they stayed and I didn't. Probably they felt equally guilty for staying when I didn't, even though neither of us had anything to do with it.

Now I've been laid off so many times that I've become hardened. Twice in 2022 is my new personal best (or worst, depending on how you look at it). The only advantage is that I've managed to separate my confidence and self-worth from my job. I don't take it personally anymore, as a betrayal or proof of my incompetence. The job loses me just as much as I lose the job.

But what I take with me are all the good memories, the relationships I've built, newfound competence and experiences, both good and bad. I don't say goodbye to everyone who stays. I say: See you soon.

Next time I meet an old friend, I should instead focus on the person, ask how they're doing and really listen. Who knows, maybe we'll find much more exciting things to talk about than work.

#job #titles #meaningfulconversations

You're staring at a blank white page and nothing happens. Zero inspiration, zero production. Empty head and empty page. What do you do?"
""
"The first and most important rule is not to think twice. Just start and write. You don't even need to know what about. Write whatever comes to mind. First just nonsense, then words that don't connect, then sentences that have no relation to each other. It's completely irrelevant whether it's good or not. You shouldn't waste time evaluating, rating, scrutinizing or judging. Set a goal for number of pages or a certain amount of time. Keep going until it's done."
""
"The reason for writer's block is that you're trying to evaluate and edit at the same time as you're producing. These are two different ways of thinking, with two different goals. Just like when driving a car, it's a bad idea to step on the gas and brake simultaneously."
""
"By starting to produce garbage, we trick the system into shutting off the inner critic. You already know it's garbage, no point wasting energy on evaluating. Since the goal was just to write, you've also succeeded and get a positive boost. High five. ✋"
""
"Now that you're already at it and writing, you can just keep going and start increasingly connecting it to what you actually wanted to write about. If you get stuck, jump to a new paragraph and write something else. Another thought or more garbage. Write Forrest write."
""
"When you're done with the page or time's up, you can switch to the editing role. Now it's the complete opposite. You should evaluate, delete, change, rearrange and reorganize. But you are NOT allowed to produce completely new text. Even if it itches. Your only task is to make the best of the material that already exists. Write a comment to your author-self instead. Set a timer, get out the digital scissors and go. Then switch again. Gas OR brake. Not both."
""
"#writersblock #creativity #writing

Life feels great when everything goes according to plan, money flows in and the wind fills our sails. Finally we've gotten a return on our hard work and we can just sit back and enjoy the success."
""
"When things are going downhill, life just rolls on. There's no reason to start investigating whether the situation is due to our efforts or just lucky circumstances. We take success as proof of our competence and the result of our initiatives. If we just continue in the same direction it will go even better if possible. It's a distorted way of thinking, also called success bias."
""
"Another downside of success is that we easily become defensive and reactive. Companies that are at the top stop innovating and instead start defending their coffers. More energy is spent fighting competitors than creating more value for users. Customers are treated however, it's so easy to get new ones."
""
"In favorable winds it's also hard to motivate change work. Money flows in as it should. Don't fix what works, people usually say. I agree that it's stupid to make something good worse, to kill the golden goose to get at the gold. What is important, however, is to understand how and why it works well and for whom. Then we can continue to build on the advantages, create new ones or be able to deliver value to more new target groups."
""
"What goes up must come down. Sooner or later the magical spring will run dry, competitors will catch up or times will get harder. If so far we've just drifted along with favorable winds without having learned to sail, we'll have a very tough time when the wind turns."
""
"#success #successbias #innovation #sailing

The camera is perhaps the most important aspect when we buy a new phone. Filming and photographing are a natural part of our everyday lives, both to document and to communicate. We've all become photographers thanks to technology removing all the tedious steps. No more developing, blurry images, or time-consuming post-processing on the computer. The mobile camera does everything automatically without you even knowing it, before, during, and after you take the picture. Images sort themselves nicely into albums, tagged and ready. All so you can avoid the boring stuff.

Photography is now about telling stories instead of technique. A personal image is more important than craftsmanship quality. When all images are technically correct, they become similar. Perfection quickly becomes tedious. No wonder there's a counter-trend emerging—taking blurry and pixelated images with an old digital camera. Suddenly your images become personal again.

This may be a temporary trend, but it shows that what we want to communicate or express doesn't lie in the technique or craftsmanship skill. Just as everyone became photographers with a new iPhone, now everyone is a designer, illustrator, and writer with Midjourney and ChatGPT. Why waste time on a bunch of Skillshare courses when you can create something nice directly with a few prompts?

That question will be difficult to answer in the near future. We need to find a new balance between what we humans are good at and how we can best harness the power of AI. Who did what becomes irrelevant. Sooner or later we'll fall back to the fundamental question:

How can we tell a personal story that touches people?

#design #digital #ai #midjourney

Whether you succeed or not is determined more by your (and others') expectations than by the result. Getting a "Pass" on a test means you've passed it. A successful outcome, in other words. But if you had your sights set on getting everything right, you see it as a failure. Same result, different consequences.

When we set expectations, we try to steer the future in a certain direction using nothing but thought. Expectations are not actions, strategies or plans. They are not aspirational like hopes. They are prophecies just sitting there waiting to be fulfilled.

Yet we can't help but set expectations. We expect a new iPhone to be better than our old one, expect a new feature to make the product better, expect our hard work to pay off. Correctly set expectations drive us forward, create energy and momentum.

That's exactly what the crux is. The right expectations are constructive, the wrong ones cause problems and disappointment. Unfortunately, they're confusingly similar, at least at first. Here are five pieces of advice to help you along the way:

Something is going to happen, you just don't know what.
The result can be either good, bad, or a mix. You usually can't know which in advance. You can, however:
- Hope for the best
- Be prepared for the worst
- Feel satisfied with the middle (at least it wasn't the worst)

Set realistic expectations together
Your customers or users have their own expectations about what value your collaboration or product should deliver. It's up to you to make sure these expectations are stated, made concrete and aligned.

Acceptance
Influence what you can influence, accept the rest. So simple on paper, so hard in reality.

Don't let others' expectations guide you
You didn't become a lawyer, football pro or brain surgeon like your family expected. Too bad. For them. You have your own life to live.

Nobody expects the Spanish inquisition
Let life surprise you. Watch a film you've never heard of, eat food you've never tasted before, talk to people without asking where they live, work or come from.
#expectation #happiness #mindful #satisfaction #energy

I'm walking around thinking about how AI will affect the way I see and practice design. So I asked ChatGPT and I think the answer is pretty good. Do you agree?"

"What are the advantages and disadvantages with using AI in UX design?"

"Advantages:"
"- AI can help UX designers to automate some of the repetitive and time-consuming tasks such as creating wireframes and prototypes, which can save time and increase productivity."
"- AI can analyze user data and behavior patterns to provide insights that can inform the design process and improve user experience."
"- AI can be used to personalize user experiences based on individual preferences and behavior patterns, which can lead to better engagement and increased user satisfaction."
"- AI can help designers to identify and fix potential usability issues before they become problems for users."

"Disadvantages:"
"- AI still has limitations in terms of creativity and human intuition, which can be important in designing intuitive and emotionally engaging user experiences."
"- AI algorithms can be biased based on the data they are trained on, which can result in unintentional discrimination or exclusion of certain user groups."
"- AI requires significant investment in terms of infrastructure, training, and maintenance, which can be costly for some organizations."
"- There are ethical considerations around the use of AI in UX design, such as privacy concerns and the potential impact on jobs and human labor."

Our primitive mind plays trick on us and interprets mundane situations like feedback as emergencies. Unfortunately we often fall into the trap and automatically react with flee or fight action. A simple question about your design choices can feel like an attack on your personality and questioning your value as a person. "
""
"It takes effort to deliberately ignore that first instinct, to calm your racing heart and take a step back. Distance yourself from the subject that is being discussed. By taking yourself out of “your design”, it becomes just “a design” that can be viewed objectively. Now you can respond in an articulate, neutral and constructive way. This takes time and practice, but the reward is definitely worth it. "
""

Everyone has an opinion about design. Nice, ugly, like it, don't like it. But going deeper than that is hard. What in the design is making you feel in a certain way? What parts are good and what parts can be improved? To translate feelings to arguments and constructive feedback is not easy. It takes training and experience. Not even designers are always good at the talking part. "
""
"Start with asking questions"
"The best way to learn about design is to talk about design. Show curiosity and that you care. Ask your local designers how and why they made certain decisions. What other options did they try? What worked, what didn’t and why? What would happen if you changed this or that?"
""
"Problem instead of solutions"
"Don’t give designers precise instruction on what to change unless you are absolutely sure that it will solve the problem. Even then you need to give reason for how you think and make room for other solutions. "
"So instead of asking designers to make the logo bigger 😉, try to articulate your concerns and ask for ways to solve them. Are you presenting your personal opinion or is it supported by data, research or opinions of others. "
""
"Need more help?"
"I will be happy to continue this conversation with you. Just reach out and we can chat. Don’t be a stranger.  "
""
"If you need help in your project with improving conversations, communication or the good old UX, I’m always here for you. Let's start a great conversation. "

It takes training and experience. Not even designers are always good at the talking part. "

"Start with asking questions"
"The best way to learn about design is to talk about design. Show curiosity and that you care. Ask your local designers how and why they made certain decisions. What other options did they try? What worked, what didn’t and why? What would happen if you changed this or that?"
""
"Problem instead of solutions"
"Don’t give designers precise instruction on what to change unless you are absolutely sure that it will solve the problem. Even then you need to give reason for how you think and make room for other solutions. "
"So instead of asking designers to make the logo bigger 😉, try to articulate your concerns and ask for ways to solve them. Are you presenting your personal opinion or is it supported by data, research or opinions of others. "
""
"Need more help?"
"I will be happy to continue this conversation with you. Just reach out and we can chat. Don’t be a stranger.  "
""
"If you need help in your project with improving conversations, communication or the good old UX, I’m always here for you. Let's start a great conversation. "
"#design #ux #conversation #feedback

Suddenly I find myself counting fingers on every picture I’m about to like. AGI is not only threatening our jobs but the sense of reality and trust. I feel cheated. Not because the output is bad, just the opposite. It creates inner insecurity about the material I’m interacting with, its origin and intent. I feel confused, without even knowing why. Probably because my old patterns and biases suddenly don’t apply. I can’t take shortcuts. What used to take milliseconds now demand effort and risk. "
""
"We are entering a world with multiple realities without almost no borders or distinctions between them. Probably we will adapt. Just as there is no clear distinction between online and IRL, the reality and alternative reality will blend to some king of semi-reality. Both the dream and reality at the same time. Maybe my future best friend will not even exist in the real world but will be as real to me as a physical person. "
""
"Until then I’ll keep counting fingers. 😅🖐 "
""
"#jobs #future #ai #reality #agi

This question is actually not about your design. It is about you and your attention seeking ego. I have this ego as well, so I know how it is. ;-)"
"Your design should be about solving problems, not worry if people like it or not. "
""
"No matter how sweet the praise tastes, it doesn't provide any feedback. Start asking relevant questions that provide information about how to improve your design, understand your users and make you grow as a designer. "
"Some examples:"
""
"- Does the design solve a certain problem?"
"- Is it easy to understand?"
"- Does it communicate what I want?"
"- Would you want to use this service if it was real?"
""
"You can also ask open questions like:"
"- How would you improve this design?"
"- How does this design make you feel?"
""
"Do you want relevant feedback on your design, portfolio or how to handle your moody design ego? Just send me a DM."

I have never understood the phrase of work life balance. I’m makes me think of Severance, where those two are completely different things. ;-) But in real life they are not. The work is just a part part of your life where it needs to coexist with other parts, like your family time, your own time and sleep. If one part starts making trouble all others will follow. If you don’t sleep well, your work results will not be great for long. "
""
"If we just keep on talkin about this work / life balance, we are excluding a big group where the lack of work is actually causing a lot of stress. Suddenly one has a lot of time but it just makes everything even worse. Even our kids that don’t have a job yet, can experience a lot of pressure and stress at school. They can also be burned out and need to find a way to recharge. "
""
"It’s not the work it self, the working hours or demanding tasks, that are causing the harm. It’s the lack of control, inability to handle uncertainty or predict he future. So instead of trying to spilt the day in half and put different labels on it we should look how at the quality of life as whole, how rewarding it is and how we experience it. "
"#work #experience #wellbeing #balance

I used to work with a product where my main purpose was to design / produce new functionality. And that’s what we designers do. Design new stuff. But there was something missing. A certain “Groundhog Day” feeling started to emerge. Every new feature was like starting over, like adding another brick to a wall. Or was it a building. Or a bridge? Nobody new. "
""
"You now the story about two bricklayers. When asked what they are doing the first one responded “I’m a bricklayer, I’m laying bricks to feed my family”. The other one responded, “I’m a cathedral builder. I’m building a great cathedral to The Almighty.”"
""
"This is often what is missing, a vision that paint a bigger picture and inspires. Even the most mundane task becomes suddenly important. It’s not just a brick, it’s a vital part of beautiful cathedral. "
"#design #vision #motivation

I do not like that man, I must get to know him better."
"/Abraham Lincoln"
""
"This is my favourite quote of all times, that says so much in just a few words. Just change the wording a more gender neutral form and you get an universal solution to the majority of all problems. "
""
"Why is it so powerful? Because it tells me that my own thoughts and opinions are not the truth. That my likes and dislikes are mostly based on familiarity. We usually like what we know and dislike or fear what we don’t know. It tells us that the key to insights and understanding is through curiosity. We don’t even have to agree with the other person, or make them change their mind. We just need to listen and relate."
""
"Imagine how different the world would look like if we applied this way of thinking. So many conflicts and wars that could be avoided. The Avengers would be a total different movie if they all sat down with Thanos and heard his version. “Good idea, but we could probably brainstorm alternative the ways to get there. Instead of decimating the population in a snap, we could just double the resources. What do you say, big guy?""
""
"SNAP"
""
"#empathy  #change #problemsolving #curiosity #avengers

We designers take pride in our work, we feel that is representing us, our skills and values. The goal of the design is to get it approved, implemented and shipped with as few deviations from our original sketches as possible, trying to make it through all the obstacles of design reviews, user testing and requests from stakeholders. "
""
"This is at least how I used to think and truth be told, it didn’t work out so well. This is a “me against the world” perspective, either you are with me, or against me. My design either survives or dies by the hands of managers and stakeholders. I also saw myself as the expert on a mission to educate and enlighten the masses. I just needed to make the nonbelievers to see the light.  "
""
"But what if there is another way? What if your design is not the main actor but a way to communicate information? What if you stop selling and pitching your design to managers, stakeholders and clients and instead started treating them as co-creators or co-enablers? What if you the goal is not to realise YOUR design but the BEST solution for the user? It does not mean that you should let go of all your ideas and believes. Just make them part of the result not THE result. "
""
"If nothing else helps, remember this: The harsh truth is that your bosses, your company or your clients actually own your design. If you get paid, your design already belongs to someone else. "
"#design #work #stakeholders #perspective

In my early carrier I had hard time understanding how the business part of the company related to my work as a designer, or how my design impacted the business. For me, business and design were opposites, one run by numbers and the other fuelled by creativity. The first took money from customers, while the other produced value for the users. "
""
"I saw myself as some kind of Robin Hood of UX, protecting the innocent users from the greed of business people, only chasing profit and growth. When they pushed, I needed to push back. This created much frustration and unnecessary tension, especially when it was a battle I could not win. The house always wins. "
""
"It was my transition to Product design that widened my perspective. What used to be a single user flow mow became an ecosystem, where the product and the user empowered each other. The product generated value that was consumed by the user, which gave the company money that were consumed and transformed to new value. A circular win-win machine."
""
"I started to see business, design and tech as the three main components of a product. They may vary in size or emphasis in different companies but the y are always present and interacting. This perspective removed the tension and replaced it with harmony. Design needs business just as much as business needs design. "
""
"There is still much work to be done to remove the silos and mistrust between the disciplines. We designers can contribute with what we know best, to understand the needs of others and design ways to bridge those gaps. "
""
"#design #business #productdesign #productdesign #growth #colaboration #silos"
"
