Things that took me far too long to realize: You don’t need stuff to do stuff."
""
"I used to believe that to excel as a photographer, I needed the best camera in the market. Hours were spent researching, buying books, and taking classes to perfect the technicalities. However, the camera ended up collecting dust on a shelf. The same pattern emerged in other areas—seeking more creativity, responsibility, or leadership roles. The truth is, you don’t need fancy tools or titles to start."
""
"Have you found yourself in a similar loop? Investing in pricey gym memberships for a beach body or waiting for your boss to notice your potential? The reality is, you can start where you are with what you have. Want to enhance your photography skills? Begin by snapping pictures with your phone and analyzing them. "
""
"Dreaming of leadership? Start by helping others and taking more initiative. No need for titles or formal permissions. Extending your care will naturally expand your influence, something no course or gadget can match."
""
"PS: Remember, ensure your actions align with your company's policies. If not, seek permission or adjust your approach accordingly. Start now with what you have, and watch your progress soar. "
""
"#Realization #StartWithWhatYouHave #PersonalGrowth

Would you work for an AI boss?"
""
"There is a lot of talk about AI assistants being the next big thing. I agree, it sounds great, for productivity and a more smooth human to machine interaction. "
""
"Somehow, that seems to be the endpoint in the conversations. The other alternative seems to be Terminator. There is less talk about the next level after the AI assistants, the AI managers. That will not require any technological shift; they will still be assistants to the board. To the regular employee, they will be something else. "
""
"- Are we ready to take orders, be reviewed, or maybe even be fired by a machine? "
"- How should that machine represent itself in real life? "
"- As an invisible, all-knowing entity? "
"- Or a digital human, looking just like everyone else in the Zoom meeting? "
"- Would we even know the difference? "
"- Would we even care, as long as it managed the company positively and responsibly? "
""
"The transformation into the AI world will be about more then just the tech. Even before we reach AGI we need to start thinking about what we want AI to be and what part in our lives it should play. "
""
"I would really like to hear your thoughts on this "

The 5 Levels of Design: From Craft to Impact"
""
"1. The Work You Do: The Output"
"This is ground zero - the actual design work you produce. We're talking wireframes, sketches, prototypes, and components. It's usually what gets us hired (hello portfolio!) and how our day-to-day work gets evaluated. Think of it as your design craftsmanship."
"As designers, we often start here as juniors, mastering our tools and building that foundation. But here's the thing - while this level is crucial, it's just the beginning of what design can be."
""
"2. How You Work: The Process"
"Gone are the days when pretty pictures alone could tell your story. Now, it's all about the thinking behind those pixels. How did you get there? What problems were you solving? Your process reveals your understanding of user needs and shows whether you're working through creative chaos or following a thoughtful, consistent approach."
""
"3. How You Work With Others: The Teamwork Game"
"Let's be real - design is a team sport. Even if you're the lone designer in your company, you need others to bounce ideas off of and validate your work. This level is about being that person everyone wants on their project."
"It goes beyond just design skills - it's about collaboration, stepping up when needed, continuous learning, and yes, even putting away that coffee mug (yours or not!). The real question is: do people light up when they hear you're joining their team?"
""
"4. Your Business Impact"
"Here's where we zoom out and look at the bigger picture. Understanding design's role in your company isn't just nice-to-have - it's essential. Forget seeing business as the "other side." You're there to help the business serve its customers better, period. The better you understand this connection, the more impact your design work can have."
""
"5. Your Impact on Humanity"
"Sounds heavy, right? But hear me out. Every design choice you make, every company you work for, every project you take on - it all adds up. We're not just pushing pixels; we're shaping experiences that affect real people's lives. Making sure that impact is positive? That's on us."
""
"Wrapping It Up"
"There's a pattern here: you start with your craft and gradually expand your view to see the bigger picture. The cool thing is, you don't have to leave one level before moving to the next - you can work on multiple levels simultaneously. The goal? Being able to look back one day and feel proud of your impact across all these dimensions."
"

The Strategic Design Disconnect: A Personal Reflection"
""
"It hit me during a seemingly routine design review. There I was, sitting with the  startup's leadership team, reviewing their new website design. The work was polished - clean typography, modern layout, all the aesthetic boxes checked. Everyone was smiling, complimenting the junior designer's skilled execution."
""
"But when I asked about strategic fundamentals - the problem we're solving, target audience, desired outcomes - the room grew quiet. What I saw as a powerful business tool, they saw as a digital storefront that simply needed a fresh coat of paint."
""
"The gap wasn't in design skills or execution - it was in understanding design's potential as a strategic force. The designer delivered exactly what was asked for: a more modern, attractive website. And technically, there was nothing wrong with that."
""
"Yet I couldn't shake the missed opportunity. With just a bit more strategic thinking, that same website could have become a robust tool for driving business growth, supporting sales, and delivering real user value. The foundation was there - it just needed a deeper purpose."
""
"Walking away from that collaboration taught me something important: the challenge isn't just about advocating for strategic design - it's about bridging fundamentally different worldviews about design's role in business. Some see it as decoration, others as strategy, and these perspectives often talk past each other."
""
"This disconnect isn't new, but it's still holding organizations back. While there's a growing movement of designers and leaders working to elevate design's strategic role, we need more voices, more examples, and more practical approaches to bridge this gap."
""
"I'm still processing how to tackle this challenge beyond the usual LinkedIn posts. How do we help both designers and business leaders see and unlock design's full potential? How do we make strategic design thinking accessible and valuable to those who haven't yet embraced it?"
""
"What's your experience with this disconnect? Have you found effective ways to shift the conversation from aesthetics to strategy? I'd love to hear your stories and insights.

The One-Hour AI Sprint"
""
"I was admittedly anxious as I introduced the assignment to the room full of UX students. The task before them seemed almost impossible:"
"In just 60 minutes, develop a complete design solution — from problem identification and research to product requirements and UX design."
""
"Setting the Stage"
"Before launching into the sprint, I delivered a keynote addressing both the transformative potential and legitimate concerns around AI. I curated resources representing optimistic and critical perspectives."
""
"The framing was crucial "
"This experiment wasn't about "AI replacing designers" but about designers evolving their relationship with this new and emerging technology. It was about challenging the current role of the designer. The workshop positioned the students as creative directors — professionals who guide, critique, and reflect rather than executing every detail themselves. "
""
"Exceeding All Expectations"
"What happened next genuinely surprised me. Not only did every single team complete the challenge, but the quality of their outputs was remarkable. One  group developed a fully functional prototype addressing study habit challenges for neurodivergent students, backed by substantive research."
""
"Key Insights from the Trenches"
"- Productivity amplification: AI tools dramatically compressed ideation-to-visualization timeframes, allowing students to focus on strategic thinking."
"- Research strength: The AI tools particularly excelled at gathering and synthesizing research insights."
"- Current limitations: Despite impressive capabilities, the AI applications struggled with iterative UX/UI refinement."
"- Workflow integration: Students quickly recognized that AI wasn't replacing their role but transforming it."
""
"The workshop revealed exciting possibilities for integrating AI into design practice. Students discovered how these tools could elevate their thinking to more strategic levels while handling execution details while their unique human abilities—empathy, ethical judgment, and creative direction—remain irreplaceable in the design process."
""
"My sincere thanks to Stockholm University, the participating UX students, and Sharon Lindberg for this opportunity. It was fun. "
""

The AI standoff between applicants and hiring managers "
""
"IKEA got tired of all the AI-generated applications and urged candidates to stop using AI. Many companies feel the same. Nobody wants to sort among thousands of generic letters.   "
""
"Is that going to happen? Are people going to stop using AI for job applications? Not a chance. "
""
"There are several reasons for that:"
"- The current job market is a mess, and people are doing everything they can to get an upper hand. They don’t cheat because they are lazy, but because they are desperate. "
"- The companies are using AI in the hiring process, so why shouldn’t I? "
"- Applying for a position is a hard, monotonous job, with lousy feedback and no pay.  "
"- Other applicants are using it, so I must as well, just to keep up. "
"- The AI is doing a good job with minimal effort, allowing applicants to invest in quantity instead of quality. "
""
"This will only lead to an arms race where both parties will use even more AI to gain the upper hand. This dilemma is not new. Education was the first to be hit by the disruptive force of AI, making the current ways of knowledge assessment unusable. "
""
"What HR should learn from Education:"
""
"- Design your process from the assumption that EVERYONE is going to use AI. Sooner or later they will. "
"- Detection software doesn’t work, so stop using or relying on it. "
"- Transparency. Be transparent with how you are using AI in your hiring process. "
"- Clear expectations and rules. Educate the applicants, when and how it’s OK to use AI. Show good and bad examples. "
"- Look for biases in your AI tools and make sure that the sorting algorithm is not a black box. "
"- One step in your process needs to be IRL, on-site, in person. This should be the most important step, where AI will not be present (from any part). "
"- Create trust. If you play by the rules, you can demand the same from your audience. "
""
"The applicants need to put in serious work as well, learning how to use A1 in a good and productive way. The companies wants to hire you, NOT your chatbot. "
""
"Now let’s stop blaming each other and fix this negative AI spiral."

The AI Readiness Quadrant: "
"How to create AI alignment in you organisation. "
""
""If you don't start learning AI, you will soon be replaced." That's the warning from CTOs at Salesforce, Duolingo, and Fiverr. Yet employees using AI often feel they're cheating or that their work is devalued."
""
"So what are we supposed to do? Should AI stay or should AI go?"
"I see this as an alignment problem. The similarities between organizations and schools are striking—there's often misalignment between management and employees. AI maturity creates another dimension. High maturity doesn't mean that you are an AI expert, just that you use AI with intent where it creates value. "
"Let's use my favorite visualization tool: the QUADRANT."
""
"The Four Zones of AI Alignment:"
""
"1️⃣ The Vulnerable Zone (Low Org/Low Employee Readiness)"
"Traditional workflows reign and AI skills are nonexistent. Companies view AI as tomorrow's problem, becoming increasingly vulnerable as the efficiency gap widens."
"For Employees: Start small—dedicate 30 minutes daily to learning relevant AI tools."
"For Organizations: Begin with a low-risk AI pilot addressing a specific pain point."
""
"2️⃣ The Risk Zone (High Org/Low Employee Readiness)"
"Organizations invest heavily in AI while employees lack skills to leverage these tools. This isn't just about ROI—it's about human livelihoods."
"For Employees: Take ownership of your AI education immediately."
"For Organizations: Shift investment toward training and create safe spaces for experimentation."
""
"3️⃣ The Shadow AI Zone (Low Org/High Employee Readiness)"
"Employees quietly adopt AI tools while organizational policies lag behind, creating innovation but also security risks."
"For Employees: Document AI successes and approach leadership with formalization proposals."
"For Organizations: Create pathways to legitimize shadow AI use rather than shutting it down."
""
"4️⃣ The Synergy Zone (High Org/High Employee Readiness)"
"The optimal scenario. Organizations and people move in harmony toward AI-enhanced workflows, creating better experiences for employees and customers alike. "
"For Employees: Develop uniquely human skills that complement AI capabilities."
"For Organizations: Create feedback loops between AI systems and human expertise."
""
"What do you think about this model? Does it make sense? And where does your organization fall on the AI Readiness Quadrant? "

Mainly routine, doing the same things, just more efficiently. If there is something designers hate, it’s routine. Switching company or roles might help, but it’s just pretty hard in times like these. There are no simple answers for this problem. Starting your own business has new, exciting challenges, but also big risks with no guarantees of success. "
""
"Starting a company without experience"
"Young designers are struggling to get into the industry. Many junior roles are now replaced by AI, and there is fierce competition for those few jobs left. Starting your own business seems more and more like a valid option, even without the experience. It’s definitely going to be hard to find the first clients, a lot of free, hard work, and many mistakes along the way. Even if your product or business fails, you will have learned a lot more, gained business and collaboration skills, self-motivation and the ability to solve your own problems. "
""
"The change"
"Winter is coming, there is no doubt about it. We just don’t know yet what is coming or what to do about it. There are no simple answers here either, no simple trick to make you irreplaceable at work. My advice is to stay optimistic, be more open-minded, explore other options, learn new things, and just play with different scenarios. If you need to reboot, what would your life look like? What do you want to keep and what do you want to discard? "
""

Is AI Actually the One Ring? 💍 "
"(The AI dilemma)"
"I sometimes get swept up by the rapid pace of AI development and the endless new possibilities it offers. Every new demo or model feels like unlocking a superpower I just have to try. Several AI tools are deeply woven into my daily workflow—I honestly can’t imagine a day without them. The product I’m building wouldn’t even exist without AI."
""
"In other areas of life, I try to make mindful choices: I buy organic or local food, I drive a gas-powered car but avoid flying, and I do my best to choose options that have a positive (or at least neutral) impact."
""
"But when it comes to AI, I feel a bit like Boromir from The Fellowship of the Ring. I want to use this incredible power for good, but I’m not sure it’s possible. What if all my well-intended achievements are ultimately outweighed by AI’s negative side effects?"
""
"There’s no going back to a world without AI. The calls to pause development have been largely ignored. AI skills are now essential to land a job, and AI is being integrated into every product—whether it makes sense or not. Opting out is nearly impossible. And unlike in Tolkien’s story, there’s no Frodo to cast all the LLMs into Mount Doom."
""
"So what do I actually want?"
"I want the ability to make good, informed choices. I want to understand the real costs of my actions and my use of AI. I want transparency, and I want options that let us choose a slower, more sustainable path. I want to be able to do more personally, to reclaim some power from the tech giants and investors who currently hold it all."
""
"The path ahead feels more uncertain than ever. The only thing that’s becoming clearer is the risk of an environmental and economic crash long before we reach the promised land of AGI or Artificial Superintelligence."
""
"It's time for me to dive deeper into Ethical AI and learn how the ring can become what we intended it be: great power with great responsibility.

The best way to learn something is to teach it to someone else. This is well known fact, still I get surprised how true it actually is. The AI Quadrant: "
"Why Both the Hype Squad and the Doomers Are Selling You Half-Truths"
""
"There’s a 2x2 grid I keep thinking about. One axis is optimism-to-pessimism. The other is whether people are understating or overstating what AI actually does. The tech-bros and doomers are both cherry-picking from the same messy reality. The actual work is learning to navigate the jagged edges yourself instead of outsourcing your judgment to either camp. "
""
"Top right: The exaggerated optimism. "
"Home of the tech-bros. Cure cancer, solve poverty, AGI saves humanity (and also makes them rich—total coincidence, right?). The good part? It’s inspiring. It makes you imagine a future beyond our current constraints. The bad part? It’s also convenient. It justifies whatever resources and power grabs happen in the name of “national security” and “human survival.”"
""
"The bottom-right: The exaggerated pessimism. "
"Say hi to AI doomers. AI becomes Skynet, replaces your job, turns the planet into paperclips. Again, there’s something real here—the labor exploitation, environmental costs, copyright theft, and regulatory chaos are actually happening. But exaggeration paralyzes people instead of empowering them to solve the problem."
""
"Top left: The oversimplified optimism: "
"“It’s just a tool, like a pen.” Comforting, sure. But a pen can’t write your paper for you. A pen doesn’t hide what it’s doing inside a black box. The charm of this view is it makes AI less scary. The problem? It lets you stop asking questions."
""
"Bottom left: The oversimplified pessimism: "
"“It’s just a stochastic parrot, nonsense, AI slop.” The value here is warranted skepticism. The problem is it misses what AI actually is good at, right now, and ignores the speed of improvement."
""
"So who’s right? Everyone. And no one."
""
"The jagged frontier of AI (term popularized by Ethan Mollick) is that it excels at some tasks and fails spectacularly at others—often in ways nobody predicted. Right now, the only honest move is to stop letting the zealots and skeptics narrate your relationship with it. Learn what it actually does. Build your own grid. Make your own bets. Just make sure to keep exploring, with both the optimistic AND the skeptical view.

Six months of learning Python, statistics, and ML theory. Zero products shipped. Zero real users. Just tutorials and planning. My board—Pieter Levels, John Maeda, Ethan Mollick, Jason Fried, Herminia Ibarra, Roger Martin, Justin Sung, Blair Enns—people I admire and trust. "

"Pieter: "You've been learning for 6 months and shipped zero products. That's not learning, that's hiding.""

Teaching UX Turned Me Into a Learning Designer (And I Had No Idea What I Was Doing)"
""
"This fall I taught two courses for Nackademin — UX/UI and Information Architecture. With my long experience in UX/UI and product design it should have been a piece of cake. Right?"
""
"It turned out to be anything but."
""
"Knowing something is completely different than explaining it in a clear, pedagogical and interesting way.To teach UX design I needed to apply learning design. But where to start and what framework to use? Flipped classroom, Bloom's taxonomy or ADDIE?"
""
"I had so many questions, no answers, and no time to start learning learning design from scratch.AI to the rescue."
""
"I brought the UX expertise. Claude helped me structure it and build out the course in Notion. We worked through Bloom's taxonomy, applied flipped classroom mentality, refined learning objectives, designed assignments, caught gaps I'd missed. Back and forth, iteration after iteration."
"It wasn't AI doing my work. It was co-creation. I knew what I wanted to achieve, inspired by all the good teachers I've had. AI helped me build it faster and more thoroughly than I could have alone, finding gaps and making it all fit together."
""
"🤔 Here's what I learned: AI is best when you already know what good looks like."
"I couldn't have designed these courses without my UX expertise. But I also couldn't have built them this fast, this thoroughly, without Claude as a co-creator."
""
"The designers who win with AI aren't the ones striving for efficiency, doing the same things but faster. They're the ones using their expertise to build things they couldn't build before.

All those wireframes, prototypes, user flows. They were shadows on a wall. Beautiful postcards from an imagined, bright future. But someone else decided if they'd become real. Someone else built them. Or didn't."
""
"I was designing in a simulation."
"Then something shifted."
""
"I built my first actual product last month. Not a prototype. Not a handoff. A real thing that real people can use. I wrote the prompts, debugged the errors, shipped the features. The gap between "I have an idea" and "here's the thing" just... collapsed."
""
"This is what people meant when they said "designers should learn to code." I thought it was about syntax and Git commits. Turns out it's not about that at all. It's about closing the loop. Seeing your idea break in real time, fixing it, watching it work. That immediate feedback cycle."
""
"The Matrix metaphor isn't just for the image. I literally feel like I took a red pill. The old world where I hand off designs and hope they survive, that world still exists. I can see it. But I'm not in it anymore."
""
"I'm not saying everyone needs to build. But if you've ever felt that frustration, you know what I mean. The thing you designed getting lost in translation, simplified, postponed, killed. This is the unlock. AI didn't just give me a tool. It gave me the ability to close my own loops. To go from idea to real thing without waiting for someone else to make it happen."
""
"I'm documenting the whole thing. Ten weeks, ten builds, every wall I hit and how I got past it. Successes and failures, everything that actually happens when you try to build for real. First one drops Friday."
""
"What about you? Still designing in the simulation, or have you started building for real?

How to Learn AI with AI"
"I was just testing to make stuff with AI. Building small apps that broke when things got hard. When I hit a wall, I stopped and moved on. This is a good and cheap way to get started, but will only get you that far. "
"What I needed was structure. A real curriculum that would push me past the walls instead of letting me quit."
""
"Here's what I did:"
"I set up three AI advisors in Claude, each with different skills and objectives:"
"- Product development expert (what to build and why)"
"- Technical mentor (how Lovable actually works)"
"- Learning designer (how to structure lessons that stick)"
"I showed them the scattered projects I'd already built, explained my goals, and asked them to design a course."
""
"They argued with each other. They argued with me, I pushed back. What to start with, in what order, level of detail and mostly importantly, how to make it valuable and engaging. To and fro until we setled on a plan that I liked and believed in. "
""
"The result: a 10-lesson curriculum that started exactly where I was and pushed me exactly where I needed to go. Better than the $2,000 courses I'd taken before — because it was built for me, not for everyone."
""
"So does this mean schools and courses are meaningless?"
"No. Good courses give you the foundation — the basic knowledge and tools you can't skip. But they can't keep up with the pace of change or customize to where you actually are."
""
"That's where AI fills the gap. It doesn't replace structured education. It extends it. You get a custom path forward once you've got the basics."
"It still takes time and effort. But now you're in charge of your own learning journey.

There's a post going viral right now, at least in the AI community. It’s written by a developer Matt Shumer, called "Something Big Is Happening.""
""
"It’s describing the rapidly growing capabilities of agentic AI. Things we all heard before. What's new and surprising is his comparison between AI and COVID—how something can be bubbling underneath the surface while most people don't see it coming. All the signs are there. We just don't want to believe them."
""
"The AI advancements aren't slowing down. They're accelerating. Things that were impossible yesterday are expected today. And the gap between what's possible and what people are prepared for? That gap is widening."
""
"Here's what worries me:"
"My students aren't ready for what's coming. Not because they're not smart or hardworking. But because the curriculum moves slowly, and AI is moving fast."
"What used to be a strength—stable, time-tested education—is now a risk. The promise schools make is: "We'll give you the tools, skills, and mindset to start your career." But if those tools are already outdated by graduation, that promise breaks."
""
"I'm not blaming the schools. Curriculum design takes time. Approval processes take time. Institutional change takes time. That used to work. Now the technology is changing faster than the process can adapt. That's the disconnect."
""
"What developers are experiencing right now:"
"Shumer describes telling AI what he wants built, walking away for hours, and returning to find it done. Better than he would have done it himself. This isn't future speculation—this is his Monday morning. Developers watched AI go from "helpful assistant" to "does my job" in about eighteen months."
""
"Designers, UX people, analysts, writers—they haven't experienced that radical shift yet. But they will. The question isn't if. It's when."
""
"Why I'm building what I'm building:"
"I can't change institutional timelines. But I can teach what students actually need right now. That's why I'm building my AI course for designers. Not to replace what schools do, but to fill the gap between what's taught and what's required. To give students—and working professionals—a way to adapt before the shift hits their field. "
""
"Because here's the truth: the people who figure this out in time will have options. The ones who wait will be scrambling.

Why the "UX Designer" role is disappearing"
""
"The LinkedIn is filled with UX people panicking about being redundant or accusing companies of not caring about users. "
""
"I have a different perspective: ”UX Designer" was never the destination. It was a solution to a problem we no longer have."
""
"Think about how the UX-role emerged. Products got complex. Teams grew. No single person could own the whole thing anymore, so we divided it up. UX here. UI there. Research in a separate room. Front-end over the wall. Everyone did their piece and handed it to the next person, hoping the original intent survived the translation. The specialization made sense. It was a necessary response to scale and constraint."
""
"But somewhere along the way we forgot it was a workaround. We turned it into an identity. A reason to stop at the wireframe and call it done. "Now we design the user experience. Hooray! ""
""
"The designers thriving right now are treating UX as strategic problem solving, not deliverable production, not just building legos with design systems. What AI can't do (at least not yet) is judge. It can't taste. It can't understand context the way someone who has met real users. "
""
"The (AI) tools now let one person be strategic, do the research, sketch the flows, build a coded prototype almost indistinguishable from the final product, and ship it. The constraint that created the UX silo is dissolving. So the label is dissolving with it."
""
"That's what Shopify and Duolingo understood. They didn't remove UX thinking — it's baked into everything they do. They removed the fence that said UX thinking stops here and someone else takes over."
""
"The workaround is over. The real work just got bigger.

Should designers, I mean developers learn how to code?"
"Wait what? Of course developers should learn to code. what kind of stupid question is that?"
"- Yesterday the answer would be YES, without a doubt."
"- Today, it’s still a Yes with a caveat."
"- Tomorrow it will be a Maybe."
""
"Take a car for example. You need a driving license to drive it but don’t need to become a mechanic. Understanding how your car works means you will be a better driver and a better car owner. You will make sure your car is in great condition, know what things you can fix by yourself and what needs an expert mechanic."
""
"Leaning to code will still be valuable, but not necessary. Writing code by hand is quickly becoming an exception. Be able to read and understand code, the structures and how it works. That are the skills that are now more valuable than pushing out more lines of code. This is not a hypothetical future. Look at Anthropic or Spotify, with almost 0 manual coding. Todays outliers are the norms of tomorrow. "
""
"The question isn't "Should designers or developers learn to code?" "
"The question is: "How much do I need to know to work effectively with AI?" "
"And the answer is: Enough to be dangerous. Enough to diagnose. Enough to drive.

"I made a prototype and it failed.""
""Nice! Congratulations! What did you learn?""
""
"That's a completely normal conversation in UX. Failure is the point. You build to discover what work and what doesn't. BOTH are equally important. Your test, learn, iterate. "
""
"Now replace "prototype" with "AI pilot" and watch the room panic."
"95% of AI pilot projects fail, says ONE study. Apparently this is a crisis. The AI sky is falling. 😱 "
""
"Here is the thing. "
"The actual problem isn't the failure rate. It's the expectation. Companies treat AI like a magic pill — slap it onto existing workflows, skip the messy middle, wait for money to rain from the sky. Then act surprised when a test behaves like a test."
""
"A pilot is a prototype. It's supposed to break. That's what it's for."
"Stop mourning the 95%. Get obsessed with the 5% that worked. Find out exactly why. Then build from there."
""
"That's how UX has always worked. AI is no different. We already had Lean Startup and Lean UX. Now it's time for Lean AI.

It's been 10 months since I left my last design job, and I still think of myself as a designer. Just not the kind UX job ads are looking for anymore."
"I opened three senior UX roles this week. All of them asked for AI skills like proficiency in Figma Make, using ChatGPT to go faster, and confidence in AI-assisted wireframing."
""
"That's not wrong. It's just describing 2019 design work with a 2026 plugin installed."
""
"Here's what's actually changed for me and how I use AI:"
"👉 As a tool: I tell it to do specific things so I can focus on the parts that need me. "
"👉 As a co-worker: I ask for opinions, pushback, a second read on decisions I'm not sure about. "
"👉 As a mentor: to teach me things I don't know, fill gaps I'd normally paper over, challenge assumptions I've held for years."
""
"That last one mattered most when I started teaching and hit a wall I didn't see coming. My instinct as a product designer is always to remove friction: smoother flows, less resistance, faster to the outcome."
"But learning doesn't work that way, the friction IS the point. The right amount of resistance triggers thought, builds memory, and makes knowledge stick. I needed someone to challenge that old reflex. So I built a dedicated thinking partner: a combined pedagogy and UX expert I could work through the course with. Lesson by lesson, asking why the order mattered, where I was making it too easy, too hard, or too complex. Learning how to stitch separate lessons into something that holds together for both the learner and the school."
""
"What job ads don't ask, and probably should:"
"How do you produce more value with AI, not just more output? "
"How does AI make you a better designer, not just a faster one? "
"And how do you delegate without giving away your thinking, your principles, or the human touch?"
""
"Those are the questions I'm figuring out in real time. Harder than learning a new tool. Far more interesting.

"We laughed and moved on. But I keep thinking about it. I believe it's not a meme, it's a prophecy. "
""
"I use AI to help me write. I research faster, structure better, get unstuck when I'm staring at a blank page. The output is more polished than it would be without it. My AI-cowriter points out inconsistencies, redundancies or when I'm too passive aggressive (yep, that happens). "
"But I know that somewhere out there, someone is probably using AI to summarize this post into three bullet points."
""
"Which means there's a real chance that what I spent an hour thinking about gets dissolved into a sentence. Fed into the next person's context. Forgotten by the algorithm before anyone actually read it."
""
"I started writing on LinkedIn to think out loud, to inspire and find people thinking about similar things. That still happens — someone writes a personal comment or a DM that makes me reconsider something. Those moments feel real and makes it worth while. "
""
"But mostly I'm stuck with this question: "
"Am I writing to a person, or am I just feeding the algorithm?

Amy Webb just killed the trend report. "
"Long live the Convergence Outlook. "
""
"Watching her annual SXSW presentation used to be my highlight of the year. No better way to get a glimpse of the future — the hopeful and the dystopian. So hearing her say it was over hit me hard."
"What she replaced it with is better. Instead of tracking individual trends, she now tracks convergences — multiple forces colliding into something irreversible. Less trend report, more storm tracker."
""
"Three insights that moved my perspective: "
"1. Your body is becoming a product surface. "
"Exoskeletons, AR glasses, gene editing. Some people will be objectively more capable than others. Opting out — for money, values, or health — will increasingly mean falling behind. We've been designing for a neutral user. That user is disappearing."
""
"2. Unlimited labor breaks the job story. "
"Agentic AI and lights-out factories produce output without people. GDP up, unemployment up — at the same time. The next internet is being built for agents to transact with each other, with humans designed out. Interaction design becomes interaction governance."
""
"3. Loneliness is a market. "
"Dependency is the product. LLMs are already the largest mental health provider in the US. Platforms don't just want your attention — they want to own how you feel before you think, vote, or buy. Emotional regulation isn't a UX side effect. It's a design surface. And almost nobody is designing it responsibly."
""
"Watch the full presentation. Download the Convergence Outlook free at ftsg.com. "
"Both are long. "
"Both are worth it."
"Thank me later.

Agents can help you build the wrong thing really, really fast."
""
"Everywhere you look, people are bragging about how many agents they have running while they sleep. Building everything and anything. If you're not burning tokens, you're falling behind."
""
"I resisted the urge. Went old school instead. Talking to humans about human needs. No prototype, no agents running overnight. Just 40 conversations."
""
"Turned out I was quite a bit off. My original idea was a solution to a problem, just not a good one. It lacked real differentiation and a strong reason to switch."
""
"What replaced it is cleaner, more focused, and honestly easier to build. I now know exactly what I'm solving, for whom, and why. The research didn't just inform the product. It became the product."
""
"Talking to real people used to be table stakes. Now it's buried somewhere between AI-generated personas, synthetic research, and the pressure to ship something, anything."
""
"Agents can build fast. But fast in the wrong direction is still wrong."
"Suddenly talk is not cheap anymore. Building is.

So many words are just words, when listening to AI(first) entrepreneurs. 😅 "
"Happy AI Easter everyone. 🐣 "
"https://lnkd.in/dfWvWHUK

All those promises on LinkedIn: "
""Build this in 2 minutes." "
""What used to take days now takes minutes.""
""
"On the surface, great. On closer look, two problems."
""
"Problem A: It doesn't actually work as shown. "
"The setup is more complicated. The result is more simplistic. It doesn't fit your process, your team's tools, the careful workflows you've spent years refining. The AI tool that promised to save you hours is unpredictable in ways the demo never showed."
""
"Problem B: It works exactly as promised."
"Wait. Wasn't that the goal?"
"Yes. But the moment something is genuinely that easy, everyone can do it. In 2 minutes. With no prior knowledge. So what exactly are you selling? When the app stores are flooded with the same idea built by a thousand different people who all watched the same demo, the effort is zero and so is the value."
""
"I've been building my own product with these tools. The parts that used to take me the longest, the layout, the boilerplate, the repetitive logic, the AI handles those now. "
""
"Fine. But I still spend most of my time on the judgment calls. Knowing what to ask for. Recognizing when the output is plausible but wrong. Deciding what matters."
""
"That part doesn't compress. It might actually be getting harder, because now there's more output to evaluate and fewer obvious errors to catch."
"Time compression is real. You will get faster. That part of the promise holds. But speed without judgment is just faster noise."
""
"The tricky part is not finding the right AI tool. It's knowing when efficiency tips over into deficiency.

Who you gonna call? "
"(For honest feedback)"
""
"Colleagues are too polite, clients too careful."
""
"The ones who give me brutally honest feedback are my wife and kids. But when it comes to how good I am at interviewing people, they're just not in the room."
"I've done over 20 research interviews now. At some point I stopped and asked: am I actually any good at this? I already knew one thing about myself. I talk too much. Fine for filling a silence, terrible when your whole job is to shut up and listen."
""
"But what else? What am I doing well? What should I keep and what should I change? Without honest feedback I'm just guessing."
""
"So I asked my second omniscience. Claude has sat in on every interview. It knows the questions, the context, the patterns across all 20+ conversations. I asked it straight: what am I doing well and what do I need to work on?"
""
"What you get depends entirely on what you ask for. Ask for a summary and you get a polite summary. Ask for the uncomfortable version and you get that instead. The AI is not going to volunteer the hard stuff. You have to actually want it. And I wanted it."
""
"The part that stuck: "You're not asking the second question. When someone says something sharp, you move on. That's where the insight actually lives.""
"I read that three times. It was right. The interviewee can't see it. I can't see it mid-session. You need someone who's been in every conversation and isn't in any of them."
""
"A machine has no stake in your reaction. No relationship to protect. It just tells you what it sees. Don't treat it like a cheerleader. It can be your toughest critic and your most honest colleague. You just have to ask the right question."
""
"I ain't afraid of no feedback.

"Now stop asking clarification questions and run the damn lab. Tasks 3 and 4. Report back what the loss curves look like.""
""
"Apparently even my AI tutor Jean-Claude can get tired of me asking too many questions. 😬 😅

I spent six weeks interviewing 22 people—designers looking for jobs, recruiters trying to hire them, and hiring managers caught in the middle. What I found was a system breaking down under its own weight, hurting everyone it's supposed to serve."
""
"My goal is simple: to make the hiring process a little more transparent, a little more human, and a little more worthy of the people on both sides of it."
""
"The full research is still worked on and will be published next week. In meanwhile I'll share some highlights and main insights.

title: Are we stuck in an AI loop, AI loop, AI loop?
text:
Are we stuck in an AI loop, AI loop, AI loop?
Are we stuck in an AI loop, AI loop, AI loop?
Created on 2026-04-21 09:14
Published on 2026-04-21 09:44
Part 1.
Candidates using AI to write cover letters, picking out the right keywords, hoping that just the pure amount of applications sent will help them land the job they want. Nobody really knows what it takes to pass the first sorting. They try out everything just to see what sticks. Even if all criteria are met, there is no guarantee that your application will make it to the next step. The bar is invisible, and the feedback signal is almost always absent. What's left is just to try out everything and hope for the best. It feels more like a lottery than a system that promotes merit.
Many candidates I've spoken to don't even want to use AI because they feel their voice is getting lost. At the same time, they don't want to be left behind when they suspect everyone else is using AI and getting an upper hand. They suspect recruiters are using AI for screening too, making it impossible to know who they're actually talking to. Is it a human or a machine? In the worst cases, both sides show up performing — the candidate rehearsing answers, the recruiter reading AI-generated questions from a screen.
What we get is candidates creating an abundance of applications just to hope to get through, making recruiters use AI to sort out the vast majority on unclear premises. Everyone is worse off. Neither side can stop.
When applying for jobs was cumbersome and hard, and writing a good personal letter took real effort, it was a sign of dedication. A personal letter could actually tell quite a lot about who you are and whether you are a good fit for the role. Now this signal has disappeared. Those who use AI carelessly lose their personality in the process. Those who still write by hand can't be trusted either — everything is suspected to be AI slop and has therefore lost its value. Meanwhile, the recruiters who are actually making hires have mostly abandoned the formal system altogether. They're working their personal networks instead.
The system gets overwhelmed by noise. The good is mixed with the bad, sorting it out takes enormous effort, and the personal skills that actually matter most are the hardest to communicate. Ghost has become so normal that one candidate I interviewed stopped categorizing it — no tag, no color code, just silence filed away as expected.
Everyone feels stuck in this hamster wheel. Candidates are busy trying to survive, find the job, and just keep afloat. Hiring managers are swamped. They don't have time or the mandate to recreate the whole system. They don't like it, but they don't see any other way. That's the most common thing I heard — on both sides.
Both sides are using AI to survive a system they wish didn't exist. It's like watching people build better weapons to fight in a war nobody wants.
I'm not anti-AI. I use it constantly. But I'm watching two communities optimize their way into mutual distrust, and it's hard to see the endgame.
The AI arms race in hiring isn't a technical problem. It's a design problem. The system incentivizes the wrong behavior. Until that changes, more AI will just mean better gaming, not better matches.
This series comes from six weeks of interviews — 22 conversations with designers looking for work, recruiters trying to hire them, and hiring managers caught in the middle. I'm sharing what I learned because I believe both sides deserve a better process. One built on transparency, dignity, and actual human contact.
Next Part 2: https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/part-2-everyone-knows-its-broken-nobody-owns-fix-boris-kehr-x0e9f/
The final report:
https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7454466805110104064/
Part 2 of my insight series is up. Everyone knows hiring is broken. This one is about why nobody fixes it — and why the system actually works perfectly for the wrong things."

title: Part 2: Everyone knows it's broken. Nobody owns the fix.
text:
Part 2: Everyone knows it's broken. Nobody owns the fix.
Part 2: Everyone knows it's broken. Nobody owns the fix.
Created on 2026-04-22 15:49
Published on 2026-04-22 15:59
There is no single villain here. No one bad policy, no one bad decision. The hiring system stays broken partly because the pain isn't distributed evenly.
The failure lands in different places
For candidates, it fails loudly. Silence where a decision should be. Rejection templates that say nothing. An invisible bar that keeps moving. For companies, it fails softly. When great candidates fall through the cracks, that loss rarely gets attributed to "the hiring interface." It gets filed under the market, salary expectations, employer brand, senior talent shortage. Problems that live somewhere else, owned by someone else. The people with the power to redesign the process rarely feel the urgency to do so.
Volume looks like opportunity until it becomes noise
More applicants sounds like a feature. More choice should mean better matching. But when 200 to 500 people apply fast, many with AI assistance, hiring stops being evaluation and becomes throughput. Criteria quietly shift: job titles, years of experience, brand-name companies, keyword matches. Shallow filters aren't what anyone wants. They're what scale demands.
The job spec is often the first thing that breaks
Several of the recruiters I interviewed told me a version of the same blunt thing: the job ad is frequently a weak artifact of an unfinished internal conversation. The role isn't defined as a business outcome. The problem isn't scoped. Tradeoffs aren't agreed on. So recruiting begins on a moving target, and once the process starts, requirements shift mid-funnel. From the outside, this reads as randomness. From the inside, it's uncertainty being managed in public. More applicants don't help here. If you don't know what good looks like, more options just mean more confusion.
When everything is AI-written, nothing is a signal
Once people believe the first filter is automated, the rational move is to write for the machine. Candidates tune CVs for ATS. They generate cover letters that "sound right", filling in all the keywords just to pass the filters. Recruiters get texts that feel generic and start distrusting all of it. Both sides become more defensive: candidates apply more because the feedback loop is dead, recruiters filter harder because the pile is unmanageable. The process becomes a conversation between tools, with humans doing cleanup on both ends.
Nobody has the mandate to stop the machine
Candidates can't stop. People are paying rent and trying to keep momentum. Recruiters are screaming, not screening. Hiring managers feel the cost of bad hires but don't own the tooling. Leadership optimizes for speed and risk reduction. Even when everyone agrees it's broken, the system keeps running because stopping it isn't an option anyone has.
The platforms profit from this without being the villain. More applications looks like growth. More engagement looks like success. When hiring gets harder, the platform becomes more necessary. The infrastructure does what it's rewarded for: activity, not accuracy.
I'm not anti-AI. I use it constantly. But we've built a system where AI makes the judgments and humans do the waiting. That's backwards. The fix isn't less AI. It's a different interface between people. One that rewards context over keywords.
This is the part two in my series about the hiring process.

title: 3. The Promised Land That Wasn't
text:
3. The Promised Land That Wasn't
3. The Promised Land That Wasn't
Created on 2026-04-24 11:17
Published on 2026-04-24 11:23
I used to tell people UX design was a good career bet.
Good money. Remote-friendly. Creative but structured. No computer science degree required. Lot of job opportunities. For a while, the market rewarded that pitch. New UX educations, bootcamps and online courses popped up all over the place. The promised land got crowded fast.
A market that built more supply than it could absorb
Junior designers competed with mid-level candidates competed with seniors who had been laid off competed with career changers who had finished a bootcamp three months earlier. The pool of designers tripled while the amount of jobs stayed the same.
Naturally companies raised the bar. Eight years required experience, earlier the max, now became the floor. Just to keep the volume down.
As one recruiter said:
"If you write five years in the ad, you get applicants with three. Write eight, you get applicants with five. The numbers have lost meaning. It is an arms race with job requirements."
A.I. enters the stage
Design and creativity were seen as the only thing machines couldn't do. Until they did.
While established designers spent months debating whether AI was worth using, the newcomers embraced it immediately. AI let them punch above their weight with minimal effort. Generating visual concepts, producing wireframes that look polished, writing synthetic UX copy, producing case study text from rough notes — all became faster and cheaper.
The surface layer is what most hiring processes actually evaluate. A portfolio that looks sharp can now be produced by someone with three months of experience and the right prompts. The signal that hiring managers already struggled to read got noisier at exactly the moment the supply of designers peaked.
The portfolio that used to be a strong signal about your skills and thinking could now be fabricated and generated. Spotting the difference between something real — with genuine afterthoughts and real-world compromises — versus a well-generated portfolio, became really hard.
Only in later stages, when you actually need to present your cases, describe your why, all the failed attempts, twists and turns during the project, only then does it become obvious if there is something behind the shiny artifacts.
This problem is not hard — it is wicked.
A hard problem has a solution if you work at it long enough. A wicked problem is one where every attempted fix shifts the burden somewhere else and creates a new problem downstream. It is when a system is part of other systems, where problems and solutions are intertwined.
There is no single solution. Especially when there are other factors that nobody can foresee or control — wars, economic tendencies, the speed at which AI can help or replace designers, companies taking safe bets, replacing workers with AI, or stopping hiring juniors.
When looking backward, it all kind of makes sense: Good times when everybody needed designers led to schools and bootcamps rushing to fill the gap, led to a flooded market and companies raising the bar, candidates using AI to get through, requiring hiring managers to do the same to filter the avalanche of applications.
And here we are.
What the research showed
What surprised me the most from all the conversations was the agreement. Both sides described the same broken experience from opposite ends. Both want fewer, better interactions. Both feel trapped in a system they did not design and cannot unilaterally change.
The system optimizes for activity. Volume. Throughput. Not for matches.
AI is used as a crutch by both parties, not as a constructive tool that brings out the best on both sides. Both sides use it, but not well. More AI is the answer to nobody. Nor are more tests, more steps in the process, or harder requirements.
We need to find a way to make the process more transparent and more human. Leave the administration, tables, and numbers to the machines and focus on conversations and relations. We need to stop treating the hiring process as finding the right set of skills to fix a problem. Instead, treat it as the start of a relationship where a person joins a group with the intent to create something amazing together.
That gap between what the system rewards and what both sides actually want is the design problem hiding inside the market problem.
This is the third and the last in my series about the hiring process.

title: Bad UX made me do it
text:
Bad UX made me do it
Bad UX made me do it
Created on 2026-04-29 16:15
Published on 2026-04-29 16:23
Last year I spent almost a full year trying to find a new job.
I thought it would be straightforward. Twenty years of experience, a decent network, solid portfolio. I was completely wrong. I got ghosted more times than I could count. Hit walls I could not see. And somewhere along the way I stopped feeling like a senior professional looking for a new opportunity rather like an irrelevant has-been that nobody needed.
Rage against the machine
The anger came next. I needed someone to blame. The recruiter. The algorithm. The job market. The AI. None of it stuck, because none of it was quite right. Eventually I got tired of being angry and decided to switch focus. I wrote about it and it became one of my most recognized things I have ever published on LinkedIn. Apparently a lot of people were sitting with the same feeling and just needed someone to say it out loud.
Stop chasing waterfalls
I stopped chasing design jobs and enrolled in AI and machine learning instead. Suddenly I had purpose and agency. Instead of writing meaningless application I wrote code, learned the math and principles behind what we lazily call AI. I learned more in a few months than for the past years.
Don’t look back in anger
A year later, with less desperation and more curiosity, I went back in. Two months of interviews with 20+ people across all sides of the hiring process. Trying to find the shape of the thing and understand how all parts fit together.
Wicked games
Hiring is a wicked problem. And what is happening to designers in this market is not one thing going wrong. It is many things colliding: the economics of design work shifting, AI rewriting what the job actually is, companies pausing on juniors, then seniors, then everyone while they figure out what a design team even looks like now. Wars, recessions, a post-pandemic hangover, organizations rethinking everything from the ground up. None of those things in isolation explains what happened to me or to the people I interviewed. All of them together do.
What I did not expect to find was hope.
Designers getting creative about how they show their value. Staying visible, building things, refusing to disappear quietly. Hiring managers pushing back against full automation, keeping humans in the decision loop, delegating the administration to the machine instead of the judgment. Small moves, but the right ones.
Here comes the sun
I do not know if this report fixes anything. That was never the goal. The goal was to understand it well enough to stop feeling like it was just me, and maybe to make the conversation a little more honest on both sides. That felt worth two months of unpaid work on a Sunday afternoon.
Thank you to everyone who talked to me. You are the report.
If you haven't yet, read the full Ghosted report at
designloop.se

title: Should hiring be more like dating?
text:
Should hiring be more like dating?
Should hiring be more like dating?
Created on 2026-05-06 11:41
Published on 2026-05-06 11:57
One question kept popping up while I was doing the interviews for the Ghosted Report. This can't be a unique problem. There need to be others struggling with exactly the same thing.
"What other area spends this much energy trying to connect two humans to see if they are a perfect match?"
So I called Olga Engwall to find out. She is a former UX colleague, now building MinglMe, a with fresh take on dating. I needed to know: have they actually solved the problem of translating data into feelings? Is there a way to use AI for more than just efficiency and automation? Or are we all just stuck in the same trap?
I needed a new perspective. I got more than one.
Olga has spent years thinking about why people connect, and more specifically, why the systems we built to help them connect keep getting in the way. Her diagnosis of dating apps is sharp: they turned human connection into a volume problem. Swipe left, swipe right, five hundred people processed before breakfast. The apps make it feel like efficiency. What it actually produces is numbness.
Every major dating app is owned by the same company, Match Group, and they all have the same basic design underneath the different branding. Some sell themselves as casual, some as serious. All of them keep you in the app as long as possible, because that is how they make money. A match is actually bad for the business model. Like a medicine that does not want to get you healthy, just keep you taking more of it.
Sounds familiar?
The Ghosted report found the same mechanics running through hiring. Candidates send applications into the void. Recruiters drown in noise they cannot convert into anything useful. Both sides perform for a system that serves neither of them. Olga summary was insightful.
"You hire A person without THE person. The personality gets removed from the process."
It is a good line. I heard similar versions of it from designers and recruiters who had never met her.
Dating apps let you filter on height, age, interests, distance. Hiring does the same with years of experience, job titles, keyword matching. Both create the feeling of control while completely bypassing the thing that actually decides whether it works: what happens when two people are in a room together.
Olga cited research on this. Even a perfect match on Big Five personality scores, the most scientifically validated personality model we have, tells you almost nothing about chemistry. Two people can score identically across all five dimensions and still sit across from each other and feel absolutely nothing.
You cannot quantify the moment it clicks.
MingleMe is built on exactly this idea. Instead of matching people on profiles and hoping for the best, the app puts people in shared activities. Cooking class, escape room, pub quiz, whatever. You buy a ticket. The chat opens. You show up. The activity is the thing, not the date. You are busy for an hour doing something together, and in that hour you see things no profile could ever show: how someone handles losing a game, how they treat a stranger, whether they are generous or stingy with praise. You cannot keep the front window up for ninety minutes while also trying to crack a code with four people you just met.
Olga's phrase for what this creates: "You see who they actually are."
A designer I interviewed for the Ghosted report, without knowing anything about MinglMe, said almost the same thing about his first in-person visit at a company he later joined. He saw the office. He watched how people greeted each other in the corridor. He got a coffee and felt whether this place could be part of his life. The formal interview mattered less than those first twenty minutes of just being there. A one designer put it:
"You taste the coffee. That tells you more than anything they ask you."
Two people from completely different worlds landing on the same metaphor. That is not a coincidence. That is a signal.
There are no soulmates
Both in dating and hiring there is this illusion of the perfect candidate. One person who solves everything. Your job is just to find them.
In reality, people with totally different skills, personality, and ways of thinking can all solve the same problem, but have completely different effects on the people around them and the organization they join. Not better or worse. Just different. One might strengthen what is already there. One might challenge it. One might bring something the job description never thought to ask for.
Most hiring processes are designed to fill a role as written. Olga is arguing for something harder: look past the current problem to where you are actually heading. The difference is between filling a position and making a bet on a direction, where the direction matters more than the starting point.
That thinking is not in any ATS system I have ever seen.
Hard skills are the entry ticket, not the destination. Your diploma, your experience, your portfolio: they prove you can do the job. But they are backwards-looking. Like stocks, you cannot judge future performance only by looking at history. At some point you need to get into the room, see if you connect, find out whether you are actually heading in the same direction.
We do not marry someone who looks good on paper. We do not become close colleagues with someone we do not like or cannot collaborate with. When there is a genuine will to connect, the other problems become solvable.
I started this conversation looking for confirmation. I already believed the hiring system was broken and I wanted another perspective on why.
What I found was someone who had stopped asking that question. Olga is not trying to improve the filtering machinery. She is not building a better swipe. She looked at the problem and asked something different: how do we get people into a real situation together, as fast as possible?
That question does not exist in hiring yet.
It probably should.
The Ghosted report is at
designloop.se
. Olga Engwall is co-founder of
MinglMe
.

Next time I will make the hiring manager do a personality test just to get an interview with me. I swear to God."
""
"Applying for simple jobs should be just that: simple. I just spent 30 minutes answering 200 questions to apply for a warehouse job. I have a forklift license. I can lift heavy things. I show up on time. That should be enough. Don't trust me? Let me show you."
""
"Before anyone read my CV, before anyone looked at my cover letter, I was asked to prove my personality was the right kind. Spontaneous or structured? Risk-taker or rule-follower? Predictable or creative? I answered honestly, knowing that was probably a stupid choice. But I want to feel that I can be myself, that there is no wrong personality."
""
"The verdict came back. And I saw directly that they were never going to hire me. My ADHD made me sound like a teenager still living in his parents' basement, not able to follow any rules, just bouncing from one thing to another. And yes, once I was that person in my teens, when I didn't know what neurodiversity was. ADHD made me behave in unpredictable ways. It is still part of my personality — but it doesn't mean I can't handle a regular job, that I don't know how to follow rules, that I don't show up on time, or that I'm untrustworthy."
""
"All of that stuff that is natural for others, I had to learn the hard way. Learn the consequences. Learn how to operate under stress. How to be organized, count time, deliver what you promised. I can do all of those things now, no problem. But ask me what I prefer: strict rules or flexibility? No question. I know which one I prefer. I also know which one is the right answer. So there I am, staring at the screen, knowing the "correct" click, wondering if I'm applying for a job or auditioning for a personality I don't have."
""
"The system was never designed to include me. It was designed for normies, and me not being one means I need to pretend. But here's the thing that really gets me: every job posting says the same thing. We embrace diversity. Everyone is welcome. All personalities valued. Then they run every single applicant through the same personality keyhole before a human has even glanced at the CV. That's not embracing diversity. That's performing it."
""
"So what's the solution?"
"We keep pretending, or we let AI fill in the answers for us. Or — and this is what I'm actually asking for — employers stop using these tests, at least not as step zero for every applicant. There is plenty of scientific evidence they don't even work. Save them for the final round if you must. But stop making someone with a forklift license prove their soul before anyone's read their name.
